The subscriber listens on core.event_bus.VL_SWITCH_POST_CASCADE (already emitted from core/vl_switch.py after each VL change applies its cascade tiers), reads preset_change_mode from prefs, and schedules 'DROP_REAPPLY' only when the active mode matches. panel.py::draw() keeps the ALWAYS_SYNC branch because its documented intent is genuinely continuous. Architectural rule enforced: bpy.ops calls never originate from draw(). See doc/troubleshooting/drop_reapply_flood_per_redraw.md.
  6. Switching the Inspector to Channels view actually shows the channels. TKS_OT_ToggleInspectorMode.invoke in operators/inspector/ops_inspector_misc.py flipped wm.tks_inspector_show_channels between True/False but never touched the sub-mode enum wm.tks_channel_mode (default 'SLOTTED'). _draw_channel_content in panels/inspector/panel.py:116 reads tks_channel_mode and branches: 'CHANNELS' draws panels/channels/draw.draw_channels, anything else draws the Scene / World / Object slotted sections. Net: clicking the LIGHTPROBE_PLANE channels-view button entered the channel-view container but stayed on the slotted layout the user had left it on. Fix: when the operator turns tks_inspector_show_channels ON for self.mode == 'CHANNELS', also force wm.tks_channel_mode = 'CHANNELS'. Toggling channels view OFF is unchanged — the sub-mode is preserved so a later re-enter doesn't override an explicit tks.toggle_channel_mode flip the user might have made.
  7. tks.delete_tag_item no longer hard-crashes Blender via unbounded Python recursion. Same bug pattern as Fixed #2 (tks.tag_add_new): commit f0da905 did a careless text-rename and pointed the single-tag delete dispatcher in TKS_OT_DeleteTagItem.execute (operators/tags/ops_groups_query.py:262) at the operator's own bl_idname (return bpy.ops.tks.delete_tag_item()), replacing the original target bpy.ops.tks.roadmap_remove_tag when the roadmap subsystem was removed. Recursion overflowed Blender's native C stack before any Python RecursionError could surface, so deleting any tag — most visibly an OUTPUT-category "rule" tag — silently terminated the process. Replaced the recursive self-call with an inline single-tag deletion that mirrors the batch-delete branch already in the same execute(): walk lib.categories for info['category_type'], select cat.tags (or cat.groups[<info['group_name']>].tags for grouped tags), target_collection.remove(info['tag_index']), clamp cat.active_tag_index, sync_tag_tree(context). See doc/troubleshooting/tag_delete_self_recursion_crash.md.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
1. Camera Visibility Automation — only the active VL's camera is visible, hands-free. A new automation under Preferences → Workflow → Automations → Camera Visibility Automation keeps the outliner and viewport clean across view-layer switches. The master toggle activates the behavior; three granular sub-flags configure it: Show Assigned Camera clears hide_viewport, hide_render, and the per-VL eye icon on the cascade-resolved camera every time the view layer changes — even if the user had manually hidden it. Hide Other Cameras stamps the same three flags TRUE on every other CAMERA object in the active scene, so non-assigned cameras disappear from outliner, viewport, and render. Affect Containing Collection (nested under Show Assigned) additionally unhides the camera's direct host LayerCollection — clearing exclude (the Exclude-from-View-Layer checkbox), LayerCollection.hide_viewport (per-VL eye), and Collection.hide_viewport (global monitor icon). The host-reveal is bounded to one collection and never walks up the ancestor chain, so a hidden grandparent collection can keep the camera invisible by design (the user retains structural control). When the camera is linked into multiple collections, the first host whose ancestor chain is already fully visible wins; if none qualifies, the first host found is used. The whole feature is hooked into the cascade hot-path right after scene.camera = camera, short-circuits with zero cost when the master toggle is off, and persists across Blender restarts via addon prefs autosave. A Reset Camera Visibility operator clears all addon-stamped hide flags on every camera in the active scene without flipping any toggle — useful when you want to peek and then let the next VL switch re-isolate.
  2. Master toggle mirrored in the Takes Tree Settings popover. A new Camera Visibility row in the existing popover (above Previews) binds directly to the prefs master toggle, so you can switch the automation on or off from the same place you adjust Collection Visibility — no detour through Preferences. The granular sub-flags stay in prefs (set up once and leave them); the popover stays minimal. When the master toggle is on, the popover also surfaces the Reset button.
  3. Single-click cascade picker — list-at-cursor for every cascade icon. Plain left-click on an inline Action / World / Camera / Compositor / Output Rule icon now opens a native popup_menu at the cursor with the sorted list of available items (compositor node trees, world datablocks, scene cameras, automation-rule tags, etc.). The currently-assigned item is rendered depressed for at-a-glance feedback; clicking any item assigns and auto-closes the popup. Above the list, the popup shows (Clear), Create New (where a tier-specific _new operator exists — auto-assigns the freshly created datablock to the target via the picker's stashed context), and Rename '' (when an assignment exists and the underlying datablock is renameable). Modifier scheme: Shift+click still opens the existing full popover (rules + presets + push-to-selected + inherited source); Alt+click / Shift+Alt+click still clear / cascade-clear as before. Camera at VL level: the existing "select the assigned camera in viewport" gesture moved from Shift+click to Ctrl+Shift+click to free Shift for the full popover, matching the other props. Implemented as a generic _picker.py module (TKS_MT_CascadePicker, TKS_OT_SetCascadeProp, cascade_picker_invoke) that the four other props route through; Compositor keeps its dedicated TKS_MT_CompositorPicker since it was the proof-of-concept. The popup uses Blender's native menu chrome rather than an addon-rendered invoke_popup, so it visually matches stock dropdowns. The rename dialog is launched via a dedicated tks.picker_rename_deferred op because invoking invoke_props_dialog directly from a popup_menu callback dispatches unreliably; the wrapper replicates tks.casic_rename's rename + casic-string-update logic so the dispatch chain is one hop deep.
  4. Cascade Overrides popover redesign — inline editors instead of stacked floating popovers. The … overflow popover (shown for rows where icons are collapsed into the single chevron button) now mirrors the inline icon row: one flat icon per enabled cascade prop with the same active/dim/alert states, in the same canonical order. Click an icon to expand its editor inline within the popover (no second floating popup). For Action / World / Camera / Output Rule the inline editor calls the corresponding sub-popover operator's draw() via a duck-type wrapper, so the picker / rule / preset / push-to-selected widgets render natively without re-implementing each editor. Compositor uses the shared popover_draw helper directly. The active icon shows the pressed/blue background while its sub-popover is expanded. Alt+click any inner icon clears every assignment for that prop at this cascade level — direct value, rule, and preset together (Output Rule also wipes all five preset fields). Icons pinned outside collapse are excluded from the popover (they live inline on the row); the popover gates on tks_show_X AND NOT tks_pin_X to avoid duplicating pinned icons.
  5. "Never" collapse mode in Tree Design popover. Third option alongside Dynamic and Always on the Collapse row. When Never is active, right-side icons never collapse into the overflow chevron regardless of panel width — the user keeps full inline control at any list width. The Pin Outside Collapse box grays out in this mode since pinning has no effect when nothing overflows. Default stays Dynamic; the existing two-button row layout becomes a three-button row (Dynamic | Always | Never) so muscle memory for Dynamic and Always is preserved.
  6. Right-click context menu for takes-tree rows. Ctrl+RightMouse on a row in the 3D View N-panel opens a context menu with a header naming the active row (VL: green, SG: Shot 01, etc.) and a Show Overrides… entry that fires the cascade overrides popover for that row. Plain RightMouse was attempted first but is consumed by higher-priority Blender keymaps (UI button context menus, 3D-View RMB select), so Ctrl is added to claim an otherwise-unbound slot. The wrapper operator is poll-guarded on area.type == 'VIEW_3D' AND region.type == 'UI' AND active takes row exists, so the binding doesn't fire elsewhere in Blender's N-panels. The same menu is the natural place to grow other row-level actions later (Clear All Overrides, Copy / Paste Settings, etc.).
  7. Workflow > Automations preferences split into collapsible categories. The flat list of ~10 automation toggles is now grouped under six TRIA-expandable boxes — Rest State, Actions, Cameras, World, Previews, Render Presets — matching the box-and-triangle pattern used by Interface > Column Visibility. Each header is the same draw_collapsible_section helper the rest of the addon uses, so the look is native Blender (icon + title row inside a layout.box(), indented content column on expand). Six new expand_auto_* BoolProperty annotations on LoggingPropsMixin persist the collapse state to the addon prefs; default is collapsed. The Cameras box also gets a nested-indent treatment for the Camera Visibility master toggle and its three sub-flags (Show Assigned Camera → Affect Containing Collection; Hide Other Cameras), so the sub-rows visually read as children of the master via a row + separator(factor=PREFS_INDENT) + column indent helper.
  8. Workflow > Sync preferences mirror the same categorised layout. Viewport Tool Settings is now a collapsible category (single one today; Timeline / Autokey sync land as additional collapsibles in the same pattern). Inside, the master Enable Viewport Sync toggle sits at the top with the seven mirror sub-flags (Transform / Snap / Proportional / Mirror / Auto-Merge / Lock Object Mode / UV) indented one level beneath via the same indent helper used in Automations > Cameras. The two informational labels ("Mirror these across all scenes:", "Last-write-wins: …") have been removed — the per-toggle descriptions already convey the same intent. One new expand_sync_viewport BoolProperty.
  9. Compositor picker plain-click popup is sized to the content with an inline search field. Replaces bpy.ops.wm.search_single_menu (which has a fixed Blender-internal width that's too wide for short comp lists). New TKS_OT_CompositorSearchPopup opens via window_manager.invoke_popup(width=N) where N is computed at invoke time from the longest comp name (clamped 180–420 px) so a single-char name doesn't shrink the popup unreadably and a 100-char name doesn't blow past the screen. Top of the popup is a prop_search-style filter field (magnifying-glass icon, bound to WindowManager.tks_comp_picker_filter); below it, alphabetically-sorted COMPOSITING node groups render as emboss=False borderless rows so they read as list items rather than buttons. The active comp is drawn depress=True; the Clear action sits as a borderless footer row below a horizontal line separator. Filter text resets every time the popup opens.
  10. N-Panel warning when the active .blend was saved by a different addon MAJOR.MINOR — surfaces the "already-fixed bug reappears after addon update" failure mode. A save_pre handler stamps scene["tks_addon_version"] with the current ADDON_VERSION on every scene; a load_post handler reads the active scene back, compares MAJOR.MINOR (patch bumps stay silent), and sets wm.tks_cache_stale / wm.tks_cache_stale_from accordingly. On mismatch a PACKAGE icon toggle joins the warns row at the top-right of the N-Panel, alert-styled exactly like the other warning toggles and gated by the same mutual-exclusivity callback (_warn_props). Clicking it expands a Cache Version Mismatch subpanel below the nav bar — header row, info box with the offending version string, and a Rebuild Cache & Reload Addon action button. The button clears every in-memory cache the addon owns (rest_state.cache.invalidate_all, rest_cache.clear_cache, preset_cache.clear_caches, preset_engine.invalidate_preset_cache, panel.invalidate_nav_warnings), re-stamps the active scene, force-redraws, then schedules bpy.ops.preferences.addon_disable → addon_enable on a 0.1 s bpy.app.timers callback so the addon tears itself down past the operator return without ripping out the operator class mid-execute. Cache reset alone had been observed to leave the takes tree in a broken state, so the reload is bundled into the fix rather than offered as a separate optional step. The addon's unregister() already calls purge_modules(), so sys.modules is wiped before re-enable for a clean re-import. The stamp lives in a Scene IDProperty (not a registered StringProperty on a PropertyGroup) because IDProps survive PG schema changes — which is exactly the churn the stamp is meant to detect, so storing the stamp in a PG would be circular.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
1. Cascade icon brightness now reflects every assignment type at every tier. Previously the inline icons used per-tier checks that were inconsistent — Camera at VL/VLG/VLV/SG/SCENE/GLOBAL was missing the *_camera_rule branch; SG/VLG world was missing tks_world_preset. After this pass, every cascade icon brightens when ANY of (direct value / rule / preset) is set, with the right tier-prefix (tks_global_* at Global, tks_scene_* for Scene presets, tks_* elsewhere). The cascade_queries.scene_has_camera() and global_has_camera() helpers now include the previously-missing tks_camera_rule / tks_global_camera_rule checks too.
  2. Overflow … chevron brightness now reflects only the icons that live in the popover. Previously the chevron lit up for any assignment on the row, even ones pinned outside collapse (where the pinned icon already showed its own bright state). Added an any_unpinned_active(wm, has_dict) helper in tree_layout_calc.py and updated all five tier any_active blocks (Global / SG / Scene / VLG / VL / VLV) to gate each contribution by NOT tks_pin_X, so a fully-pinned-outside row whose only assignments live on pinned icons no longer keeps the chevron lit.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
1. Addon no longer writes to Blender's user keyconfig — full non-interference with other addons and user customs. v0.7.3 stopped the Nav and Render Scope pies from polluting user kc, but the Keyframe pies on I / Alt+I still ran keymap_items.new() on wm.keyconfigs.user.keymaps['Window'] because Blender 5.x's Window/EMPTY keymap dispatch is unreliable for custom operator entries in addon kc alone. That bootstrap created is_user_defined=True entries in the Window keymap, and Blender's "rebuild user keyconfig on addon enable" pass on every cold restart drops all is_user_defined=True kmis in addon-touched keymaps — collateral damage to any other addon's user-kc overrides or user shortcuts that happen to live in Window. The keyframe pies now register in wm.keyconfigs.addon only, matching the rest of the addon. Active-flag sync on Blender's auto-mirror entry still happens (modifying an existing kmi doesn't change is_user_defined), so cold-start dispatch works. Trade-off: if Blender's auto-mirror doesn't sustain a custom-operator kmi across an in-session Reload Addon action (it sustains wm.call_menu_pie reliably but not tks.invoke_keyframe_pie), the keyframe pies may stop firing until the next full Blender restart. Accepted in favour of a clean user keymap — the addon is now a citizen that only touches its own keyconfig.
  2. Foreign-write and continuous-state instrumentation in the keymap-audit module. Used to bisect the non-interference fix. A trace_foreign_write() chokepoint logs [FOREIGN-WRITE] whenever the addon mutates a kmi outside the tks.* / wm.call_menu_pie namespace (only the intentional native-I / Alt+I deactivations should trigger this in normal operation). A bpy.app.timers periodic monitor at 2.5 s emits [AUDIT-DIFF] periodic-tick lines when keyconfig state changes between register / save / load boundaries, catching mutations from pref toggles or sibling-addon writes that aren't already wrapped by an audit phase. Both gated by the UI:HOTKEYS debug topic — useful for any future regression report.
  3. Group-level Compositor "Create New" no longer crashes with a TypeError. TKS_OT_GroupCompositorNew.execute() called find_scene_group(master, group_name) — a 2-arg call to a 1-arg function (find_scene_group(scene) looks up the SG that contains a given scene, not by name). Pre-existing bug that was only reachable via the new Create-from-picker entry on Scene Group rows; replaced with an explicit name lookup over get_scene_groups().
  4. Compositor picker classes were imported but never registered. TKS_MT_CompositorPicker and TKS_OT_SetCascadeCompositor were listed in panels/popovers/__init__.py::_misc_classes, but that file's register() is not in the addon's master register chain — the canonical class list lives in panels/context/_class_registry.py, which didn't include them. Net effect: clicking the compositor cascade icon opened a wm.search_single_menu against a menu idname that didn't exist in RNA, producing an empty popup. Both classes (plus the new TKS_OT_CompositorSearchPopup wrapper) are now in _class_registry.py's imports and classes list, and re-exported from panels/context_popovers.py so the import chain is consistent. The picker now actually lists the compositor node groups.
  5. tks_comp_picker_target_type WindowManager property moved above the class-registration loop in panels/context/__init__.py::register(). Previously sat after the for cls in classes: loop, so a failure mid-loop left the popover operator alive (since some operators register from a different module's classes list) but the property unbound — manifesting as AttributeError: 'WindowManager' object has no attribute 'tks_comp_picker_target_type' on the next compositor picker invoke. Moved alongside the related tks_comp_filtered collection so a class-loop hiccup can never strand it again. Defensive change; the underlying class-registration failure is fixed separately by entry #4.
  6. Camera Visibility automation now fires on group-tier camera assignments. VL Group / Scene Group / VL Version tks_camera StringProperty annotations have no update= callback by design — assigning a camera via the picker at one of those tiers used to silently write the value with no cascade refire, so the Camera Visibility hook (which lives inside apply_overrides()) never ran until the next VL switch. The generic picker setter TKS_OT_SetCascadeProp.execute() now explicitly calls apply_overrides(prop_filter=CascadeProperty.CAMERA) whenever prop_type == 'CAMERA'. Idempotent for VL / Scene / Global tiers (those already refire via their own update= callbacks), bulletproof for the group tiers that don't.
  7. Cascade icons (the red broken-state badge in particular) refresh instantly after a picker assignment. The icon-state cache _vl_cascade_cache in panels/context/draw_layers.py was only invalidated by the Scene / VL / Global update= callbacks (via _invalidate_and_redraw()) plus the ~200 ms context sync timer. Assigning at SG / VLG / VLV tiers — which don't have update callbacks — left the stale entry in place; the icon kept its old (often red/broken) color until the next sync tick. TKS_OT_SetCascadeProp.execute() now calls invalidate_vl_cascade_cache() plus signal_redraw() after every picker assignment regardless of prop type, since all four pickers (ACTION / WORLD / CAMERA / OUTPUT_RULE) share the same icon-state cache.
  8. Active takes-tree row no longer jumps to the last item after a cascade picker assignment. Assigning a value through the generic picker setter could leave wm.tks_all_viewlayers_index pointing somewhere unexpected — most visibly with ACTION assignments where the property write triggers depsgraph.update() + a context-sync-driven structural rebuild whose selected-key lookup occasionally mis-resolves. The picker click is meant to set a value on the row the user clicked, never to move the selection. TKS_OT_SetCascadeProp.execute() now snapshots the active tree index before setattr, runs the cascade refire + cache invalidation + redraw signal, and restores the saved index if it drifted (bounds-checked against the possibly-rebuilt collection, so we don't fight the system when the row genuinely vanished). Defensive — fixes the symptom for all four picker prop types (ACTION / WORLD / CAMERA / OUTPUT_RULE) regardless of which sync path is shuffling the index underneath.
  9. Scene / VL / VLG / VLV camera picker only shows cameras linked to the target scene, so the icon never stays red after a "successful" assignment. Previously the camera picker listed every CAMERA object in the file via _PICKER_CONFIG['CAMERA'].collection_fn. If the user picked a camera that lived only in another scene's collection hierarchy, the cascade engine's per-scene name in scene.objects validation in apply_overrides() (core/cascade.py:500-523) silently auto-cleared the assignment — the user saw the value briefly in the picker, then watched the cascade icon stay red because there genuinely was no usable camera at any resolved tier afterwards. New _camera_collection_fn(context) reads the picker's stashed tks_picker_scene_name + tks_picker_target_type and pre-filters to cameras in scene.objects when the target is scene-bound (Scene / VL / VLG / VLV). Scene-agnostic tiers (GLOBAL, SG) still see the full list because their cascade fans out to many scenes; the per-scene validation correctly happens at apply time there. The cascade's silent auto-clear safety net remains for stale assignments loaded from older .blend files or external scripts.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
1. Keyframe pie menus on I and Alt+I — both off by default. Two opt-in pies. The Keyframe Pie (I) covers insert/set actions (Set as Rest Default, Insert at Rest State, Snap to Rest, Snap All to Rest, Insert Keyframe (Native)), and the Clear Pie (Alt+I) covers clear/remove actions (Clear Current Keyframe, Unset Rest Key (Channel), Unset Entire Rest Slot, Clear All Keyframes (Object), Clear All Rest Keys (VL), Delete Keyframe (Native)). Each pie has its own 8-slot configurator under Preferences → Workflow → Pie Menu with mutual-exclusion and reset workflow matching the Navigation and F12 pies. Enable each master toggle independently; until you do, I and Alt+I behave exactly like stock Blender.
  2. Pie hotkeys are now rebindable from the configurator. The center cell of every pie configurator (Navigation, F12 Render, Keyframe, Clear) is a live hotkey button — click it and Blender's "press a key" prompt captures a new key + modifier combo. Want to keep native I behavior and put the Keyframe Pie on a different key? Click the center cell and press your preferred chord. The same widget works for the Ctrl+Shift+C navigation pie and the F12 render pie. A reset icon (↩) sits glued to the hotkey field as a connected sibling button — one click restores the built-in default (Ctrl+Shift+C, F12, I, Alt+I) with a "Reset hotkey to default" tooltip. The whole rebind widget is centered between the W and E slot dropdowns and falls back to a static label when the pie's master toggle is off (no keymap entry to edit yet).
  3. Hotkeys panel lists all four pie shortcuts. The Navigation, F12 Render, Keyframe, and Clear pie entries now appear inline in Preferences → Workflow → Hotkeys alongside the other Takes shortcuts, so you can review or rebind every pie from one place with the standard Blender keymap-editor controls (expand row, change modifiers, restore default).
  4. Editor Scope for the keyframe pies covers every keyframe-capable editor. Per-editor checkboxes under Preferences → Workflow → Pie Menu → Editor Scope let you opt the pies into the 3D Viewport, Properties, Graph Editor, Dope Sheet, NLA Editor, Shader Editor, Image Editor, Movie Clip Editor, Video Sequencer, and Outliner. The wrapper routes per context.area.type and falls back to the editor's native I / Alt+I (anim.keyframe_insert, anim.keyframe_insert_button, graph.keyframe_insert, clip.keyframe_insert, …) when an editor's toggle is off, so disabled editors keep native behavior.
  5. Hover-aware slot visibility. Outside the 3D Viewport, hover-dependent slots (anything that needs a specific property — Set/Insert at Rest, Snap to Rest, Clear Current Keyframe, Unset Rest Key (Channel), Insert/Delete Keyframe (Native)) only appear when the cursor is over a keyframable value. Empty canvas hides them and leaves the slot direction as a separator, so muscle memory still works for the 8 directions. The 3D Viewport always shows every slot — selection-based actions don't need hover.
  6. Three new Rest State operators. Unset Rest Key (Channel) removes a single fcurve's channel for the active property, Unset Entire Rest Slot removes the active object's slot from the Rest State action, and Clear All Rest Keys (VL) walks every object in the active view layer and clears their rest slots. All three appear as slot choices in the Clear Pie.
  7. Viewport tool settings now sync across scenes. A new Workflow → Sync → Viewport Tool Settings section lets you mirror selected 3D viewport tool settings — transform orientation + pivot + Affect-Only toggles, snap settings, proportional editing, mirror, auto-merge, lock-object-mode, UV — across every scene as you change them. Last-write-wins: any scene's change to a synced setting propagates to all the others. Each group has its own toggle, gated by a master Enable Viewport Sync switch (off by default). The msgbus subscriptions are only armed while the master is on.
  8. Auto-clearing the Scene World on an empty cascade is now optional. A new Auto-clear World when Unassigned toggle under Preferences → Workflow → Automations (on by default) controls whether scene.world gets wiped when no world is assigned anywhere in the Takes tree. With it off, the existing Scene World is preserved and the Cascade Drift warning surfaces persistently as None → <WorldName> to remind you to put a world into the tree (or accept the manual assignment).
  9. Apply Scene World to any cascade tier from the drift warning. The drift row for World now opens a tier-picker dropdown (Global / Scene Group / Scene / VL Group / View Layer / VL Version) — pick a tier and the active scene.world is written into that level's tks_world override. Tiers that don't apply for the active context (no Scene/VL Group beyond the default "Ungrouped", no active VL Version) are greyed out. Replaces the single-target Accept button for World drift; Accept still appears for Compositor drift.
  10. Undo can no longer silently destroy a manually-assigned Scene World. A new Skip World-Clear in Post-Undo Cascade required-core strategy (on by default, under Preferences → Workflow → Debug → Undo-Redo) makes the post-undo apply_overrides skip the world-clear branch entirely. Before this, undoing back to a state where no world was in the cascade tree would wipe scene.world on the very next post-undo cascade run — even if the user had assigned it outside the tree.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: 1. The Inspector Watchlist keeps a stable height when it's empty. The list and its sidebar buttons (Add / Remove / Settings / Find Slot) now always render; the empty-state info message appears as a row in the stats slot above the list instead of replacing the whole list with an info box. The panel no longer jumps in height the moment the last watched object is removed.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
1. Pie hotkey rebinds now persist across every routine pref change. Earlier 0.7.2 builds rebuilt the pie keymap entries on every Editor Scope checkbox flip, master toggle round-trip, and addon reload — wiping any custom key the user had bound via the configurator's center hotkey button or via Blender's keymap editor. _register_keymap() is now idempotent across all three pies (Navigation, F12 Render, I + Alt+I) — it preserves existing kmi entries when found and only flips the active flag to match master state. Per-editor toggles and slot dropdown changes no longer touch the keymap layer at all (the wrapper operator reads prefs dynamically). Full keymap teardown only happens on addon unregister.
  2. New codebase audit catches scope-crossing writes early. Part J of the codebase stats (doc/reports/codebase_stats.md) now scans every Python file for writes to wm.keyconfigs.user and wm.keyconfigs.default and lists them grouped by severity. Lines tagged with a # SCOPE_OK: <reason> comment are exempt and still listed for review; anything unjustified deducts from the Health Score. The 17 legitimate user-keyconfig writes in 0.7.2 (Blender 5.x dispatch workaround, master-toggle reversible flips, full-teardown removes on unregister) all carry the marker now, so the audit reads 0 unjustified scope crossings.
  3. Pie menu keymaps no longer crash Blender on per-editor toggle. Flipping a checkbox in Editor Scope (or any path that re-runs pie keymap registration) could trigger an EXCEPTION_ACCESS_VIOLATION deep inside rna_KeyMap_item_remove → BLI_findindex when a stored kmi C-pointer had been freed by Blender between cycles. The unregister path now walks each keyconfig fresh and matches entries by idname + properties, so it never dereferences a stale pointer. The same hardening is applied to the Navigation, F12 Render Scope, Global Shortcuts, Tag Groups, and Context panel keymap teardowns.
  4. Ctrl+Shift+C navigation pie and F12 render pie fire reliably in Blender 5.x. Both pies registered only in the addon keyconfig and relied on Blender to mirror entries into the user keyconfig — which Blender 5.x doesn't do reliably, so dispatch silently failed even though DIAG showed entries active=True. Each pie now registers in both keyconfigs explicitly, the same fix used for the new I-key pies.
  5. The Post-Undo Cascade Ping no longer silently aborts on scenes without a compositor node tree. A null scene.node_tree access raised a non-fatal exception that bailed the entire ping mid-scan, so any datablock not yet visited never had its cascade rebuilt. The access is now guarded so the ping continues with the rest of the scan.
  6. The render-window-close watcher fires reliably during undo bursts again. The mutation-skip guard added in 0.7.1 broke the post-undo cascade ping — it relied on this timer firing promptly during undo. The guard is removed; a K5_EXEMPT code comment plus a project audit note keep it from being reintroduced.
  7. The F12 Render Scope pie shows operator errors as status-bar messages instead of console tracebacks. When the inner batch render operator raises a RuntimeError via self.report({'ERROR'}, …) — e.g. "Save file before starting Batch Render!" — the pie's dispatcher now catches it and re-reports the message via the pie operator's own status bar.
  8. Clean Empty Slots & Actions has a combined operator, a preview dialog, and a safety pass. A single Clean Empty Slots & Actions runs both passes after showing a preview of what will be removed; an extra safety pass leaves fake-user-pinned actions and slots that are still referenced elsewhere alone.
  9. JIT rest action cleanup runs in the two merge branches that previously skipped it. Two specific just-in-time merge paths left their source actions behind without scheduling them for removal — they now follow the same cleanup schedule as every other branch, so orphan actions stop accumulating after long sessions.
  10. Preset capture covers Hydra Storm engine settings and the Output Time Stretching ratio. Render presets now save scene.hydra.* (visible under Render Properties → Hydra when the render engine is Hydra Storm — currently just Export Method). Output presets now save the Time Stretching Old / New frame-mapping ratio. Both were silently absent before.
  11. Accepting a Cascade Drift no longer crashes on World or Compositor. accept_drift was assigning a World / NodeTree datablock to vl.tks_world / vl.tks_compositor, both of which are StringProperty. The latent type-mismatch was masked by the old auto-clear-world behavior never producing a drift in the relevant case; with the world-clear toggle the drift becomes reachable. Accept now writes actual.name and clears with "" to match the property type.
  12. The Context tree view now repopulates on every file load, not only the first of a session. The _sync_timer fast-path gate is driven by module-level globals in panels/context/handlers.py (_sync_dirty/_context_sync_dirty/_last_fingerprint/_last_structure_key). They start True (first load populates) then flip False after the first sync. Being Python globals they survive File>Open, but the WM-backed tks_all_viewlayers collection is reset by the load (Load UI swaps in the file's WM) → empty collection + closed gate + no re-arm = blank tree. The structural fallback only re-arms on a scene/VL count change (a same-structure reload doesn't trip it) and World-stored group data doesn't reliably fire depsgraph_update_post on load — the exact gap _handle_undo_redo_sync already covers for undo/redo. Fix: new @persistent _handle_load_post_sync (file-load twin of the undo handler) re-arms both dirty flags, nulls _last_fingerprint/_last_structure_key, and busts TKS_UL_AllViewLayers._sg_cache_token / groups._vs_cache_token; registered/unregistered on bpy.app.handlers.load_post in panels/context/__init__.py. Tests: dev/tests/test_tree_load_refire.py (5 — flag re-arm, fingerprint/structure-key clear, load_post arg tolerance, registration). 543/543 headless. See doc/troubleshooting/tree_blank_after_second_file_load.md.

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Takes for Blender provides a deep toolset for managing complex product visualization pipelines. Each feature is designed to work together through the cascade system.

docs.title: Core
docs.text: Feature Description Takes Hierarchical organisation of Scenes, View Layers, and Versions. Cascade System 6-tier property override resolution engine. View Layer Versions Named cascade-override snapshots beneath each View Layer. Globals Panel Project-wide control centre — settings, presets overview, rules, tags, variants.

docs.title: Automation
docs.text: Feature Description Tags Universal labelling system for organisation and automation. Rules Tag-driven preset bundles applied through the cascade. Variant Switch Per-scope material swapping and product variants. Smart Output Dynamic token-based file path resolution.

docs.title: Production
docs.text: Feature Description Render Presets JSON-based presets for render, output, view layer, color, camera, world, material, bookmarks. Batch Render Foreground & background multi-View Layer rendering with queue management. Rest State Auto-mirroring rest action for preserving default keyframes. Value Lock Protects unkeyed values from accidental global changes while you work. Still Mode Pins takes to the frame-0 still frame per cascade tier — stills stay stills, turntables keep animating. View Layer Preview Inline rendered thumbnails next to each View Layer.

docs.title: Extras
docs.text: Feature Description Bookmarks Quick-access pointers to animatable properties anywhere in Blender. Pie Menus Configurable pies — navigation (Ctrl+Shift+C), F12 render, keyframe and clear. Process Monitor Runtime diagnostics — process tree, debug console, View Layer Switch profiler. Viewport Sync Keep snap, pivot, proportional-edit and other viewport tool settings identical across every scene.

docs.title: Batch Render
docs.text: The Batch Render system automates rendering across multiple View Layers, applying cascade overrides (cameras, worlds, actions, presets, variants) for each one.

docs.title: Render Modes
docs.text:
Takes for Blender supports three render modes:

ForegroundBackground Batch RenderRender Active View Layer

Renders inside the current Blender session. You see the render window and progress in real-time, but Blender is locked during rendering.

  • Open the render menu from the queue sidebar with Foreground mode selected — every scope row then runs Batch Render.
  • Progress shows per-View Layer with status indicators.
  • Press Esc to cancel.

Renders headlessly while Blender stays fully interactive. Selecting Background mode in the render menu dispatches tks.batch_render_bg_fast: the .blend is saved once, a single queue file is written, and one persistent headless Blender process works through every queued View Layer — no per-task startup cost, so large queues finish quicker. The F12 Render Pie background scopes route the same way.

  • Tree view updates progressively as each View Layer completes.
  • A completion sound plays when all tasks finish.
  • While a background batch is running the sidebar button becomes an X so you can cancel without leaving the sidebar.
  • Alt-click force-renders every View Layer even if it already completed.

Renders only the active View Layer — useful for quick spot-checks without queueing the full batch.

  • Reached from the render menu (Active Layer Only, under Other) or via Ctrl-click on a View Layer's render-toggle icon.
  • Requires the .blend to be saved.

docs.title: The Render Menu
docs.text:
The queue sidebar carries a single render button ( in Foreground mode, in Background mode) that opens the render menu. A Render Mode toggle at the top switches between Foreground and Background; every scope row below dispatches in whichever mode is selected. The scopes are grouped into three categories:

Category Row What it renders Selected Takes This Scene Every render-toggle-enabled VL in the current scene only. All Scenes Every render-toggle-enabled VL across every scene. Pick Scene Choose a specific scene (with a Search scenes… option) to render its enabled VLs. All Takes This Scene Every VL in the current scene, regardless of render-toggle. All Scenes Every VL in every scene, regardless of render-toggle. Pick Scene Choose a specific scene and force-render all of its VLs. Other Active Layer Only Just the active View Layer. Resume — Skip Done Enabled VLs across all scenes, skipping ones that already finished. Retry Failed Re-render only VLs whose previous attempt failed or cancelled. Calibrate Render Times Probe-render the queue to seed the time estimates.

The same dispatcher (tks.render_scope_dispatch) powers the F12 Render Pie, so anything you assign to a pie slot maps to one of the scope rows above. While a render or calibration is running, the sidebar button turns into its cancel button.

docs.title: Render Queue
docs.text:
The render queue shows the status of each View Layer:

Status Description Pending Waiting to be rendered. Rendering Currently being processed. Saving Writing output file to disk. Done Successfully completed. Failed Error occurred (hover for details). Cancelled Skipped due to batch cancellation.

docs.title: Queue Columns
docs.text:
Click the gear icon in the queue header to open the Queue Columns popover. It controls which info columns are visible alongside each row, with three sub-controls:

  • Icons — toggle individual columns on or off. At least two visible columns must remain so the overflow indicator is meaningful.
  • Pin Outside Collapse — pin specific icons so they always render, even when the queue auto-collapses on narrow panels.
  • Collapse — switch between Dynamic (auto-collapses when the panel is narrower than the Min Width value) and Always (always collapse).

docs.title: Calibrate Render Times
docs.text:
Click Calibrate Render Times in the queue toolbar to estimate per–View Layer runtime before you commit to a real batch. The operator:

  1. Spawns a headless Blender subprocess in the background — your foreground stays interactive.
  2. Probe-renders each queued View Layer at two reduced resolutions, with every other setting left real — samples, adaptive sampling, denoising and compositing are untouched.
  3. Fits a line through the two probe timings and extrapolates along the pixel axis alone to the View Layer's full output resolution. Extrapolating only pixels is what keeps adaptive-sampling scenes honest — a pixels-times-samples model assumes the nominal sample cap is reached, which adaptive sampling rarely does.
  4. Streams the estimates back row-by-row; each View Layer's Estimated Render Time column updates as its probe completes. View Layers that share a scene's render settings share one probe, so calibration finishes faster on typical multi-take scenes.

Re-run it any time scene changes have invalidated previous estimates (geometry rebuilt, engine swapped, samples bumped, etc.). Clicking the operator again while it's running asks for cancellation — same pattern as cancelling a background batch.

Why probe instead of measure?

A probe takes seconds per View Layer instead of minutes, so the initial estimate is necessarily approximate — but it doesn't stay that way. The ETA corrects itself while a batch runs: once the first take finishes, the remaining time reflects how this run is actually performing. Background renders additionally show the render engine's own remaining-time prediction for the take in flight. And the queue remembers each take's recent render times together with the settings they were measured at, rescaling those estimates when resolution or samples change.

docs.title: What Gets Rendered
docs.text:
The batch renderer processes every View Layer whose render-toggle icon is enabled, regardless of which row is active or selected. Active/multi-select state in the tree drives editing, not rendering.

To control the queue, toggle the render icon (next to each View Layer row):

  • Click → toggle that View Layer's enabled state.
  • Shift + click → toggle every View Layer in the same scene at once.
  • Alt + click on a render-menu scope row (or the sidebar render control) → force-render every View Layer, even completed ones (otherwise the queue skips already-rendered VLs unless Skip Completed is off).

Render Order

The batch renderer follows the tree view order (top-to-bottom as displayed), not Blender's internal scene/View Layer order.

docs.title: Recovery
docs.text:
If a batch render gets stuck:

  1. Alt+Click the Render button to force-reset.
  2. This clears all internal flags and restores suppressed handlers.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Output paths are resolved via the Smart Output token system. Each View Layer's output is named automatically based on the configured pattern.

docs.title: Detect Version From Disk
docs.text:
When your output pattern includes a {rev} version token — either in a folder segment or in the file name — the Smart Output section shows a Version field with a refresh button () beside it. Detect Version From Disk scans the resolved output folder for files and folders that already match the versioned pattern, finds the highest number present, and sets the Version field to highest + 1 so your next batch continues the sequence instead of overwriting earlier renders.

  • Click the refresh button next to Version to run the scan (operator tks.detect_render_version).
  • It checks both versioned folder names and versioned file names, picking the highest across both.
  • If nothing on disk matches the pattern, it leaves the value untouched and reports No existing versions found on disk; otherwise it reports the version it is continuing from and the highest it saw.

When to use it

Reach for this after copying a project, pulling renders from a render farm, or otherwise picking up work where the in-file version counter has drifted from what is actually on disk. It only reads the folder — it never deletes or moves existing renders.

docs.title: Hotkeys
docs.text:
The render-toggle icon next to each View Layer accepts modifier-clicks:

Shortcut Action Click Toggle the View Layer's enabled state in the queue. Alt + click Preview Render — open the View Layer's most recently rendered image into Blender's Render Result. Requires a finished render to exist; fails silently otherwise. Ctrl + click Render & save the View Layer through the queue. Shift + click Toggle all View Layers in the current scene.

While a batch render is running:

Shortcut Action Esc Cancel the batch render. The status line shows (ESC to cancel) while running. Alt + click on the Render button Force-reset a stuck render state.

See Keyboard Shortcuts for the full reference.

docs.title: Bookmarks
docs.text: Bookmarks are quick-access pointers to animatable properties scattered across Blender's UI. Bookmark a property once and it shows up as an editable row inside the Inspector's Channels view, no matter where the original control lives.

docs.title: What Gets Bookmarked
docs.text:
Any animatable property — anywhere a right-click menu offers "Bookmark Property":

  • Object transforms (location, rotation_euler, scale).
  • Modifier values.
  • Material / shader-node inputs.
  • World shader properties.
  • Custom properties (["MyProperty"]).
  • Shape keys, particle settings, and other RNA paths.

docs.title: Adding a Bookmark
docs.text:
1. Right-click any animatable property in any panel (Properties, N-panel, Geometry Nodes, etc.).
  2. Choose Bookmark Property.
  3. The bookmark appears in the Inspector's Channels view, grouped by target type.

docs.title: Removing a Bookmark
docs.text: Removal goes through the same right-click menu as adding: on a property that is already bookmarked, the menu entry flips to Delete Bookmark. That works on the property's original control and on its row inside the Channels view alike — the rows are the real properties, so they carry the same menu. There is no dedicated remove button on the bookmark row itself.

docs.title: Where Bookmarks Live
docs.text:
• Scope: per-Scene (stored as scene.tks_bookmarked_properties).
  • Persistence: saved with the .blend.
  • Visibility: shown wherever the target object / material / world is currently active.

docs.title: Bookmark Presets
docs.text: Bookmark sets can be saved as a Bookmark preset (one of the 9 preset categories — see Render Presets). Use this to share a curated channel layout across scenes or with collaborators. The preset row lives in the Channels view itself: click the bookmark icon in the Channels header to show it, then save or revert the current bookmark set like any other preset.

docs.title: Import / Export
docs.text:
Three housekeeping operators exist for moving bookmark sets between files, but they are not drawn in any panel — reach them through Blender's operator search (F3):

  • Export Bookmarks — writes the scene's bookmarks to a JSON file for sharing.
  • Import Bookmarks — reads such a file back in, merging with what's there (already-bookmarked properties are skipped, not duplicated).
  • Clear All Bookmarks — removes every bookmark from the scene, behind a confirmation dialog (toggleable in Preferences > Interface > Confirmations).

docs.title: Hotkeys
docs.text: Bookmarks have no dedicated hotkey — they're added and removed via the right-click menu on any property.

docs.title: Cascade System
docs.text: The Cascade is the core engine of Takes for Blender. It resolves property overrides through a 6-tier hierarchy, allowing any level to override any level above it.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
When you switch to a View Layer, the cascade resolves each property (camera, world, action, compositor, presets) by walking the hierarchy from the most specific tier upward and using the first non-empty value it finds. View Layer Version wins over View Layer, View Layer wins over View Layer Group, and so on up to Global as the final fallback:

graph LR
    Global[Global] --> SceneGrp[Scene Group]
    SceneGrp --> Scene[Scene]
    Scene --> LayerGroup[View Layer Group]
    LayerGroup --> Layer[View Layer]
    Layer --> Version[View Layer Version]

    style Version fill:#e87d0d,color:#fff

The arrow direction shows hierarchy (Global is the parent, View Layer Version is the leaf). Resolution priority runs in the opposite direction — leaf wins over root.

docs.title: Override Tiers
docs.text: Tier Scope Example Use Global All scenes, all View Layers Default camera, global world Scene Group All scenes in the group Shared exterior lighting Scene All View Layers in the scene Scene-specific compositor View Layer Group All View Layers in the group Shared camera angle View Layer Single View Layer Per-shot camera, action, world View Layer Version Named snapshot Version-specific tweaks

docs.title: Cascade Properties
docs.text: Two cascade resolvers live alongside each other. They walk the same 6-tier chain but answer different questions, so the wiki splits them into separate tables to match the source.

docs.title: Pointer Cascade
docs.text:
These are the cascade's "primary" properties — each one resolves a single datablock reference (or a single rule name) and is driven by the CascadeProperty enum / resolve_cascade resolver. Each shows up as its own cascade icon on every tree row.

Property Resolves to Description Camera Camera object Which camera object is used for rendering. World World datablock Which world environment is used. Compositor Node tree Which node tree drives compositing. Action Action datablock Cascade action applied to managed objects on this View Layer. Output Rule Tag name The active automation rule that drives the five output-side preset slots.

docs.title: Per-field Cascade
docs.text:
These are name-keyed preset slots and selection rules. Each one cascades independently via the generic get_cascade_winner resolver, so a Scene-level Render preset can win while a VL-level Color Management preset overrides it. The Output Popover groups the five output-side slots in one place; Camera Rule and World Rule are surfaced via the Camera and World popovers.

Slot / rule Driven by Description Render preset tks_render_preset Engine, samples, resolution. Output preset tks_output_preset Container, dimensions, frame range. File Output preset tks_fileoutput_preset Format, color depth, compression, Smart Output paths. View Layer preset tks_viewlayer_preset Active passes, light groups, holdouts. Color Management preset tks_colormanagement_preset View transform, look, exposure. Camera preset tks_camera_preset Focal length, sensor, DOF. Applied to the cascade-resolved camera. World preset tks_world_preset Background, strength, mist. Applied to the cascade-resolved world. Camera Rule tks_camera_rule Tag-based automatic camera selection. World Rule tks_world_rule Tag-based automatic world selection.

docs.title: Via Cascade Icons
docs.text: Click any cascade icon on a tree row to open its popover. Set a value to create an override at that level, or clear it to inherit from the parent.

docs.title: Via Context Properties
docs.text: The Context Properties panel shows all overrides for the active View Layer in one place.

docs.title: Creating Datablocks per Tier
docs.text:
The Action, World, Camera and Compositor popovers carry a + button () next to their picker — from the Global tier all the way down (tier coverage varies per type, see the table). Clicking it creates a brand-new datablock — auto-named from the Smart Output naming template for the tier you're on — marks it with a fake user so it survives a save/reload, and assigns it to that tier in one step. This saves you from creating a datablock in Blender's own browser and then pointing the cascade at it.

Operator Button Creates and assigns to tks.global_action_new New Global Action A new Action on the Global tier. tks.scene_action_new New Action A new Action on the Scene tier. tks.vl_action_new New Action A new Action on the View Layer tier. tks.scene_world_new New World A new World on the Scene tier. tks.vl_world_new New World A new World on the View Layer tier. tks.global_camera_new New Global Camera A new Camera on the Global tier. tks.scene_camera_new New Camera A new Camera on the Scene tier. tks.vl_camera_new New Camera A new Camera on the View Layer tier. tks.global_compositor_new New Global Compositor A new compositor node tree on the Global tier. tks.group_compositor_new New Compositor A new compositor node tree on a Scene Group, View Layer Group, or View Layer Version (one shared operator serves all three group tiers). tks.scene_compositor_new New Compositor A new compositor node tree on the Scene tier. tks.vl_compositor_new New Compositor A new compositor node tree on the View Layer tier. tks.rest_action_new New Rest Action A new Action assigned as the Rest Action (see below).

Rest Action

The New Rest Action button lives on the Rest State popover, not the cascade tier itself. The Rest Action is the snapshot that unkeyed properties fall back to: any property without a keyframe snaps to its value in the Rest Action. Creating one here gives you an empty action to pose into as your neutral / rest baseline.

docs.title: Renaming Without Breaking Anything
docs.text: Cascade assignments are stored by name, so renaming a datablock in Blender's own UI would orphan every assignment pointing at it. The popovers therefore carry a pencil button, Rename (tks.casic_rename): it renames the assigned Action / World / Camera / Compositor in a small dialog and rewrites every cascade reference to the old name across all tiers in the same step — so the rename can never break an assignment.

docs.title: Deleting Assigned Data
docs.text: Ctrl+Shift+Alt-clicking any cascade datablock icon (Action / World / Camera / Compositor, any tier) invokes Delete Data (tks.purge_assigned_data) — deliberately hidden behind a three-key chord so it can't fire by accident. Its confirmation dialog spells out the full blast radius before you commit: every cascade location the datablock is assigned in, its user count in the file, and whether fake-user protection will be overridden (cameras additionally note that only the object is deleted, its camera data stays). Confirming unassigns the datablock from every slot, then deletes it from the file.

docs.title: Visual Indicators
docs.text:
• Bright icon — A value is explicitly set at this tier
  • Dimmed icon — The value is inherited from a parent tier
  • Alt+Click — Clear the override at this tier

Cascade Debugging

Hover over a cascade icon to see a tooltip showing which tier the current value is inherited from.

docs.title: Managing the Cascade Action
docs.text:
The Action cascade is special: it doesn't just point at a datablock, it actively pushes that action onto every managed (watched) object on the View Layer. A few operators help keep that in sync:

Action Operator What it does Re-apply Cascade Action tks.reapply_cascade_action Forces the resolved cascade action back onto all watched objects, updates the depsgraph for an immediate viewport refresh, and clears the "action mismatch" entry from the navigation warnings. Use it after manually fiddling with an object's animation data. Pin Action tks.pin_action_override Locks the action currently active on an object into that object's own override, so the cascade will leave it alone instead of overwriting it on the next switch. Reports a warning if the object has no action to pin. Push to Selected tks.push_override_to_selected Copies one override value from the active Scene / View Layer to every multi-selected View Layer at once. Works for Camera, World, Action, Compositor, Output Rule, and Variant. Pushing an empty value clears that override on the targets. The button only appears while a multi-selection is active. Update Name tks.update_action_name Renames the assigned Scene- or View-Layer cascade action to match the current naming template — useful when generated action names have gone stale after renaming a scene or View Layer. No panel button; run it from Blender's operator search. Unlink Action tks.scene_action_unlink Clears the current scene's Scene-tier action assignment (the action datablock itself stays in the file). Also operator-search only — the popover's Alt-click clear is the everyday route.

Bulk editing with Push to Selected

Select several View Layers in the tree, set the value once on one of them, then use Push to Selected to fan it out — handy for giving a batch of shots the same camera or world without touching each row.

docs.title: Cross-Scene Camera Linking
docs.text:
When you assign a camera at the Global or Scene Group tier, that camera has to exist in every scene the tier covers. If it doesn't, picking it pops up the Camera not linked in all scenes confirmation (tks.link_camera_and_assign) listing the missing scenes and offering three choices:

  • Link & Assign — link the camera into each missing scene (mirroring its current collection placement) and then assign it.
  • Just Assign — keep the assignment at the source tier and let the cascade silently skip scenes where it can't resolve.
  • Cancel — do nothing.

The cascade picker also flags an incompatible camera with an error icon before you click, so you can spot the situation in advance.

docs.title: Version Variants
docs.text:
View Layer Versions can override which variant of a product is shown — this is the highest-priority tier in the Variant Switch cascade, so a version's choice wins over everything below it. The version's variant popover exposes two operators:

Action Operator What it does Set Version Variant tks.vlv_set_variant Pins a specific product to a chosen variant index on this version. If the version is currently active, the variant cascade re-applies immediately. Clear Version Variant tks.vlv_clear_variant Removes that product's variant override from the version, letting it inherit again. Re-applies live if the version is active.

docs.title: Broken Assignments
docs.text:
Because cascade assignments are stored by name, an assignment breaks when the datablock behind it disappears — deleted, or renamed outside the addon's own rename tool. The cascade can then no longer resolve that slot, and a warning sub-panel appears in the Navigation panel's warning row listing every broken reference with its data type and tier.

The panel repairs as well as reports:

  • X on an entry runs Clear Broken Assignment (tks.clear_broken_assignment), emptying the stale reference(s) to that one datablock; a type row's Empty all button clears every broken reference of that data type at once.
  • The magnifier button runs Replace (tks.replace_broken_assignment), which reopens that exact tier's cascade popover so you can point the slot at a replacement instead of clearing it.
  • Clear All at the top runs Clear All Broken Assignments (tks.clear_all_broken_assignments), emptying every broken cascade assignment in the file in one click.

Clearing only empties the stored reference — nothing is deleted — and the warning disappears as soon as every assignment resolves again.

docs.title: Cascade & Preset Drift
docs.text:
Drift is the opposite failure mode: the assignment is fine, but the live value no longer matches it — you changed the world or compositor through Blender's native UI instead of the cascade, or edited settings governed by one of the tier-cascaded preset slots (Render, Output, File Output, View Layer, Color Management, World, Camera). The addon compares the scene's current state against what the cascade last applied and lists each mismatch in a Navigation warning, with a ✓ / ↩ pair per entry:

Button Operator What it does ✓ on a World / Compositor row Accept Cascade Drift (tks.accept_cascade_drift) Adopts your manual pick — writes it into the active View Layer's assignment, so the cascade owns the new value from now on. ↩ on a World / Compositor row Revert Cascade Drift (tks.revert_cascade_drift) Discards the manual change and re-applies the cascade-resolved value. ✓ on a preset row Accept Preset Drift (tks.accept_preset_drift) Pushes the drifted preset assignment onto the active View Layer's cascade tier. ↩ on a preset row Revert Preset Drift (tks.revert_preset_drift) Restores the cascade-assigned preset.

The rule of thumb: Accept when the change was intentional and should stick to this shot; Revert when it was an accidental edit in the wrong panel. (Don't confuse this with the preset dirty state — drift is about which datablock or preset is assigned, dirty state is about edited values inside an assigned preset; see Render Presets.)

docs.title: Overflow Icon
docs.text: On narrow panels the per-row cascade icons collapse behind a single overflow indicator. Clicking it (tks.overflow_icon_click) opens the inline editor for the chosen cascade property; Alt + clicking instead clears every assignment for that property at that tier in one go — the direct value, its selection rule, and its preset slot together. The status line reports how many values were cleared (or that the slot was already empty).

docs.title: Hotkeys
docs.text:
Cascade icons on tree rows and in the Context panel respond to modifier-clicks:

Shortcut Action Click Open the popover for this property. Alt + click Clear the override at this tier (revert to inherited value). Shift + click Toggle the same property across all items of the same type in the active scene. Ctrl+Shift + click Toggle the same property globally across every scene and group. Ctrl+Shift+Alt + click Delete the assigned datablock from the file, after a blast-radius confirmation — see Deleting Assigned Data.

Datablock pickers (Camera, World, Compositor, Action) follow the same convention — Alt + click clears the assignment.

See the full reference on the Keyboard Shortcuts page.

docs.title: Custom Tokens
docs.text:
Custom Tokens let you define your own Smart Syntax tokens from live scene data — no scripting, no code. Pick a source (Scene, View Layer, Render settings, Camera, a fixed text, or an assigned Tag), tell it what to read, give it a name, and from then on [yourtoken] works in render output paths exactly like a built-in token.

They live in Preferences → Takes for Blender → Workflow → Token, alongside a read-only reference of every built-in token.

Why you'd want this

The built-in registry covers the common cases. Custom tokens cover your cases: the focal length as a bare number for lens-based folders, your studio and client names, a per-layer sample override, the color view transform in the filename of look-dev renders — anything a render path should carry that the built-ins don't.

docs.title: Tutorial — your first custom token
docs.text:
Let's build [lens], a token that resolves to the active camera's focal length (50, 85, …), and use it in a render path.

  1. Open Preferences → Takes for Blender → Workflow → Token.
  2. In Custom Tokens, click Add › Category. Name it Camera Rig, pick an icon (the icon button opens a browsable, searchable icon library), and enter lens as the first token. Confirm.
  3. Your token exists but doesn't read anything yet — click its pencil to open the editor:
    • Source: Camera — the token reads from the scene's active camera.
    • Reads: data.lens — the dotted path from the camera object to the value.
    • Format: .0f — round to a whole number (50.0 → 50).
  4. Watch the Now: line under the fields — it live-resolves while you type. When it shows Now: 50, the token works.

  5. Use it: open any render output field's Build Syntax popover and click your token in the grid (custom categories appear after the built-ins), or just type [lens] into the path:

    //renders/[scene]/[lens]mm/[proj]_[variant]_####

    renders to //renders/ShotA/50mm/MyProject_Red_0001.png.

That's the whole loop: category → token → source + reads + format → check "Now:" → use it.

docs.title: How the Reads path works
docs.text:
The path is a chain of attribute names separated by dots, starting from the source:

  • Source Camera + data.lens → camera object → its data (the camera datablock) → lens.
  • Source Scene + world.mist_settings.depth → scene → its world → mist settings → depth.

Three rules make a good path:

  1. End at a single value — a number, a text, or an on/off. A datablock at the end (like parent or focus_object) is fine too: it resolves to its name. Don't end on a list or a color — you'd get raw data dumps in your filename.
  2. Only letters, digits, dots and underscores. No brackets, no parentheses, no function calls — the token system reads values, it never runs anything.
  3. When in doubt, hover it in Blender. Enable Python Tooltips (Preferences → Interface), hover any property, and the tooltip shows its path — e.g. Camera.dof.aperture_blades. Drop the datablock prefix and use what remains, from your chosen source's point of view.

docs.title: Format specs
docs.text:
The optional Format field is a standard Python format spec applied to the value:

Spec Turns Into .0f 49.9999 50 .2f 3.14159 3.14 03d 7 007 +.1f 0.5 +0.5

A spec that doesn't fit the value is ignored rather than breaking the path.

docs.title: Sources at a glance
docs.text: Source Reads from Path example Scene the active scene unit_settings.scale_length View Layer the active view layer samples Render the scene's render settings image_settings.color_mode Camera the scene's active camera object data.lens, location.z Fixed Text nothing — the "path" field IS the value MyStudio Assigned Tag — Scene / View Layer the Tag assigned via the Tags feature (no path needed)

docs.title: Managing your tokens
docs.text:
• Editing — the pencil on a token row (Edit Token) opens the edit dialog with live validation and a Now: preview of what the token resolves to in the current scene.
  • Categories are labels — a category exists as long as one token carries it. Click a category title to rename the whole group inline (invalid names simply snap back); the pencil on the category row (Edit Category) renames it with error messages and picks its icon; a token's own Category field in the edit dialog moves just that token. Category headers expand and collapse on click (Toggle Category).
  • Icon browser — click a category's icon to browse the full icon library, grouped and searchable; picking a cell applies it (Set Category Icon).
  • ★ / 👁 — star favourites (Toggle Favourite) and hide tokens (Toggle Hidden); the Build Syntax grid can filter to favourites and built-in/custom views.
  • Deleting — the row's ✕ (Delete Token) asks for confirmation, like every other delete in the addon. The toggle lives in Preferences → Interface → Confirmations → Globals → Delete Custom Token.
  • ⚠ rows — a stored token whose name clashes with a built-in (possible via presets from older versions) shows a warning instead of a misleading preview; built-ins always win a name clash.

docs.title: Token Presets
docs.text:
The preset row at the top of the Token tab saves and loads named sets of your custom tokens (definitions + category icons), stored in the same tiered folders as render presets: Project / Local / Shared / Add-on. A set can travel with a .blend project or be shared with a team.

Two presets ship with your install if you followed the setup: Starter Pack (16 essentials) and Example Library (the full 50 below) — load either via Select in the preset row.

docs.title: Third-party addon data
docs.text:
Custom tokens can read properties that other addons register on the Scene, View Layer, Render settings, or Camera — that's most well-behaved addons. Cycles itself works this way:

  • Source Scene + cycles.device → GPU
  • Source Scene + eevee.taa_render_samples → 64
  • Source Scene + my_physics_addon.wind_strength → whatever that addon stores

Not reachable: bracket-style custom properties (from the Custom Properties panel — those aren't attributes), objects other than the active camera, and other addons' preferences.

docs.title: Safety
docs.text: Resolution is a strict read-only walk: no function is ever called, no private attribute is ever followed, and no code from the path is ever executed. A wrong path can't crash anything — the token just resolves to nothing and shows dimmed until the path is fixed.

docs.title: Example Library — 50 tokens to learn from
docs.text: Every example below is verified against live Blender data. Load them all at once via the Example Library preset, or copy just the recipes you need. Values shown are typical.

docs.title: Camera Rig (Source: Camera)
docs.text: Token Reads Format Example [lens] data.lens .0f 50 [sensor] data.sensor_width .0f 36 [cam_type] data.type PERSP [cam_height] location.z .1f 1.7 [dof_dist] data.dof.focus_distance .2f 3.20 [cam_x] location.x .1f -4.2 [clip_start] data.clip_start .2f 0.10 [clip_end] data.clip_end .0f 1000 [shift_x] data.shift_x .2f 0.00 [blades] data.dof.aperture_blades 0 [dof_on] data.dof.use_dof True [rig] parent.name CamRig (dims when unparented) [focus_obj] data.dof.focus_object.name Hero (dims when unset) [camname] data.name ShotCam.001

docs.title: Color & Look (Source: Scene)
docs.text: Token Reads Format Example [view_xform] view_settings.view_transform AgX [exposure] view_settings.exposure +.1f +0.5 [gamma] view_settings.gamma .2f 1.00 [display] display_settings.display_device sRGB

docs.title: Scene & Units (Source: Scene)
docs.text: Token Reads Format Example [worldname] world.name Sky_Dusk [unit_scale] unit_settings.scale_length .2f 1.00 [units] unit_settings.system METRIC [framestep] frame_step 1 [gravity] gravity.z .1f -9.8 [autokey] tool_settings.use_keyframe_insert_auto False

docs.title: World (Source: Scene)
docs.text: Token Reads Format Example [mist_depth] world.mist_settings.depth .0f 25 [world_nodes] world.use_nodes True

docs.title: Render Settings (Source: Render)
docs.text: Token Reads Format Example [threads] threads 16 [aspect_x] pixel_aspect_x .1f 1.0 [film_alpha] film_transparent False [persist_data] use_persistent_data True [simplify] use_simplify False [fps_base] fps_base .2f 1.00 [stamp_note] stamp_note_text approved_v2

docs.title: Output Format (Source: Render)
docs.text: Token Reads Format Example [color_mode] image_settings.color_mode RGBA [quality] image_settings.quality 90

docs.title: Engine — addon-registered properties (Source: Scene)
docs.text: Token Reads Format Example [render_device] cycles.device GPU [vp_samples] cycles.preview_samples 1024 [denoise] cycles.use_denoising True [eevee_samples] eevee.taa_render_samples 64

docs.title: View Layer (Source: View Layer)
docs.text: Token Reads Format Example [vl_samples] samples 0 [mat_override] material_override.name Clay (dims when unset) [vl_active] use True [pass_z] use_pass_z False

docs.title: Studio (Source: Fixed Text)
docs.text: Token Text Example use [studio] MyStudio brand every delivery path [client] ClientName per-client folders [proj] MyProject short project code [version_tag] v001 bump by editing one token, every path follows [dept] Lighting department / discipline folders

docs.title: Tags (Sources: Assigned Tag)
docs.text: Token Source Example [scenetag] Assigned Tag — Scene hero_shots [vltag] Assigned Tag — View Layer blue

docs.title: Putting them together
docs.text:
//renders/[proj]/[client]/[scene]_[view_layer]/[dept]/[version_tag]/[lens]mm_[view_xform]_####

resolves to something like:

//renders/MyProject/ClientName/ShotA_Beauty/Lighting/v001/50mm_AgX_0001.png

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Meaning Fix Token shows dimmed in the grid, Now: shows nothing Nothing to resolve right now (no camera, no parent, tag unassigned…) Not an error — it resolves the moment the data exists Red message under the fields The name or path is invalid (built-in clash, illegal characters, private segment) Follow the message; the token isn't saved as broken Value looks like raw data (<bpy_prop_collection…>) The path ends on a container instead of a single value Add the final step, e.g. .name ⚠ clashes with a built-in token on a row A preset from an older version loaded a name that's now built-in Rename the custom token — the built-in wins until you do

docs.title: Globals Panel
docs.text: The Globals panel is the project-wide control centre. It collects everything that's not tied to a specific Scene or View Layer: project settings, the preset library, automation rules, the tag library, and the Variant Switch tree.

docs.title: Where to Find It
docs.text: 3D Viewport > Sidebar (N) > Takes tab > Globals**.

docs.title: Modes
docs.text:
The header has a row of mode buttons. Each switches the panel body to a different view:

Mode Icon Shows Settings gear Project-wide settings (Rest Action, version warning, …). Presets preset Overview of 8 preset categories in one place. Variants UV-sync The Variant Switch tree (Products / States / Parts / Pools). See Variant Switch. Rules gear (system) Automation rules — see Rules. Tags colour mod The full tag library — see Tags.

docs.title: Settings Mode
docs.text:
Rest State controls.

Control Description Rest Action picker Selects which Action stores the rest baseline. The + button creates a new one on the fly. Auto-Mirror Keyframes toggle When on, the addon mirrors unkeyed values into the Rest Action whenever you keyframe a property.

See Rest State for the full feature description.

docs.title: Presets Mode
docs.text:
Overview of all preset categories with their assignments and dirty state.

A stacked list of 8 labeled preset rows — each with its current assignment, dirty indicator, and revert button:

Render • Output • File Output • View Layer • Color Management • Camera • World • Material

Click any preset to open its picker. Alt-click on the icon to clear. See Render Presets.

Where's Bookmark?

The ninth preset category, Bookmark, is deliberately not part of this grid — it is a render-config overview, while bookmark save / revert lives in the Channels view.

docs.title: Variants Mode
docs.text: Delegates to the Variant Switch tree (Products → States → Parts → Material Pools).

docs.title: Rules & Tags
docs.text: Dedicated panels for the Rules and Tags features.

docs.title: Version Warning
docs.text: If your Blender build is below 5.0 (the minimum for slotted actions), the Globals panel surfaces a banner with a link to the upgrade guide. The banner disappears once you're on a supported build.

docs.title: Hotkeys
docs.text:
The Globals panel shares the generic tree hotkeys with every other tree-based panel:

Shortcut Action Ctrl+N / Shift+A Add (smart / full menu). F2 Rename. Del / X Delete. Ctrl+G / Alt+G Group / ungroup (Tags & Rules). Ctrl+T Retarget. Ctrl+I Invert multi-selection.

See Keyboard Shortcuts for the complete reference.

docs.title: Pie Menus
docs.text: The addon registers four configurable pie menus — the Navigation Pie for switching between side-panel views, the F12 Render Pie (opt-in) for picking a render scope, and the opt-in Keyframe (I) and Clear (Alt+I) pies for Takes-aware keyframing, each of which can grow its own Still / Animation sub-pies. All of them share the same 8-direction layout, the same de-duplication behaviour (assigning an action that already lives in another slot blanks the previous slot), and the same in-Preferences configurator UI under Workflow > Pie & Misc.

docs.title: Navigation Pie
docs.text:
Fast switching between the addon's side-panel views without taking your hand off the mouse.

Default binding: Ctrl+Shift+C in the 3D Viewport. The pie is enabled by default.

Each cell shows the slot's compass direction and the action assigned to it. The cursor icon in the centre is where the pie opens — you flick toward the direction you want.

NWVariants NRules NETags WTree View EWatchlist SWBatch Render SSlotted Mode SEChannels

docs.title: Navigation Pie — Slot Actions
docs.text: Action What it toggles Tree View Context panel (Takes Tree). Watchlist Inspector watchlist. Slotted Mode Slotted-mode panel. Rules Globals → Rules mode. Variants Globals → Variants (Variant Switch). Tags Globals → Tags mode. Batch Render Batch Render panel. Channels Inspector → Channels view. None Empty slot.

docs.title: F12 Render Pie
docs.text:
Replaces Blender's native F12 binding with a render-scope picker — pick what you want to render (active layer, this scene, every scene, only failed, etc.) and the pie dispatches to the right batch operator.

Default binding: F12 in any window. The pie is off by default to avoid surprising existing muscle memory — flip the F12 Render Pie Menu toggle in Workflow > Pie & Misc to activate it.

Default slot layout (each slot is reassignable). The label in the centre is where the pie opens. The SE slot ships empty, ready for the action you reach for most.

NWAll Takes · This Scene NResume — Skip Done NERetry Failed WActive Layer Only ESelected Takes · This Scene SWAll Takes · All Scenes SSelected Takes · All Scenes SE(empty)

docs.title: F12 Pie — Slot Actions
docs.text:
In the slot dropdowns these actions are grouped under three headings — Selected Takes (View Layers whose render icon is on), All Takes (every View Layer, render icon ignored), and Other. Inside the pie itself the scope actions carry their group in the label (e.g. Selected Takes · This Scene).

Action What it renders Selected Takes · This Scene Every render-icon-enabled View Layer in the current scene only. Selected Takes · All Scenes Every render-icon-enabled View Layer across every scene. All Takes · This Scene Every View Layer in the current scene, regardless of render-icon state. All Takes · All Scenes Every View Layer in every scene, regardless of render-icon state. Active Layer Only Just the active View Layer (tks.render_active_vl). Resume — Skip Done Resume a batch — skips View Layers already marked done. Retry Failed Re-render only the View Layers whose previous batch attempt failed or cancelled. Native F12 Fall back to Blender's built-in render.render. Open Render Settings Switch the active Properties editor to the Output context. None Empty slot.

The same scope actions are also available from the render menu behind the queue sidebar's render button — see Batch Render. In Background mode both the pie and the menu dispatch through the fast background renderer (tks.batch_render_bg_fast), and the menu's Other category also offers Calibrate Render Times (tks.calibrate_render_times) to seed the queue's time estimates.

docs.title: Search Scenes
docs.text:
The per-scene scope submenus list every scene as its own row, which gets unwieldy in a heavily populated file. Each submenu therefore opens with a Search Scenes entry (tks.render_scene_search, shown as Search scenes…) at the top. Picking it opens Blender's type-to-filter search popup so you can find a scene by name and render it immediately — no scrolling the full list.

  • It respects the popover's current Foreground / Background choice, so the chosen scene renders in whichever mode is active.
  • It mirrors the submenu it was opened from: the plain variant renders that scene's render-toggle-enabled View Layers, while the force variant renders every View Layer in the scene regardless of toggle state.

docs.title: Keyframe & Clear Pies
docs.text: Two opt-in pies that replace Blender's I (insert keyframe) and Alt+I (clear keyframe) shortcuts in the editors you choose. Where the native shortcuts only know about ordinary keyframes, these pies put the addon's Rest State actions on the same muscle-memory gesture — set a rest default, snap back to rest, or clear rest keys without hunting through menus. Both pies are off by default: enable them in Workflow > Pie & Misc (Enable Keyframe Pie / Enable Clear Pie (Alt+I)).

docs.title: Keyframe Pie (I) — Slot Actions
docs.text:
Default layout: N Set as Rest Default, S Snap Active to Rest, W Snap All to Rest — the remaining slots ship empty.

Action What it does Set as Rest Default Insert the current value into the Rest State action at frame 0. Snap Active to Rest Snap the active property back to its rest value — no keyframe inserted.
