docs.title: Naming Presets
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: Core · Size: 🔴

Let users save their token-naming style as a named preset they can switch between, instead of hand-editing dozens of templates and risking that the cascade stops finding its objects. A preset captures just the naming settings (every template + the separator) — not the whole addon config — so you can keep all your other preferences and only swap the naming style.

The point of it all: a .blend made with a custom naming style must open and work on any other PC — including a stranger's, who doesn't have your preset and may use a completely different style. A file that uses a custom style carries that whole style inside itself, so opening it never depends on having the right preset already installed. Your local preset library is just for convenience; it's never needed to open someone else's file.

Three things make it safe:

  • A "will this resolve?" check. Before you commit a naming style, the addon tells you whether the tokens you've composed will actually let the cascade tell your scenes, view layers, versions and objects apart — or whether two of them would collapse to the same name and silently clash.
  • Migration from one preset to another. When you switch presets, offer to rename the existing actions and slots in the file from the old naming to the new one, so nothing is orphaned. Anything the addon can't confidently rename is shown to you to map by hand — it never guesses.
  • Remembering which preset a file was made with. A .blend made with a non-default naming style records which preset it used. Open it later with a different style active and the addon warns you up front and offers to switch or migrate, instead of letting the cascade quietly fail.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Save / load / list naming presets (just the naming settings, stored alongside the addon's other presets)
  • "Will it resolve?" validator — warn before a naming style is applied if it would clash or produce empty names
  • Embed the whole naming style inside any file that uses a custom one, so it opens correctly on a PC that doesn't have the preset (default-style files carry nothing — they already work everywhere)
  • On open, use the file's embedded style for that file; offer to import it as a local preset or migrate to your own — works even for files from strangers
  • Safety net on open for files with no embedded style (older files): test the file against each saved preset, adopt the one that fits, fall back to default if none do, and list what didn't fit
  • Auto-suggest the best-fitting preset (one click to accept); suggest a likely name for each leftover item, always asking before renaming
  • Migrate a file from preset A to preset B (rename actions + slots + the addon's own cameras/compositors — never the user's own objects), with a preview and a big-change confirmation showing exact counts. Creating a preset changes nothing on its own; only migrating touches the file
  • Hand-map screen for anything migration can't rename automatically
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Prefix Token Syntax
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: Core · Size: 🟡

Offer a prefix token style — $scene, $camera, $frame — as another option alongside the current bracketed styles, so the syntax feels familiar to people coming from other DCCs like Cinema 4D or Houdini. The $ sits only on the left — there is no closing mark — so the token starts at the $ and runs to the end of the token name ($scene, not $scene$), which reads a little cleaner. You pick it in preferences next to the existing bracket styles, and the token sheets and builder emit tokens in whichever style you've chosen.

  • Make the token sheets / builder emit and preview prefix-style tokens
  • Convert cleanly between prefix and bracketed styles, leaving anything that isn't a known token alone (including a literal $ in a path)

docs.title: Searchable Preferences

Shipped

docs.title: Preload Viewport Integrity
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Quality · Area: Core · Size: 🟡

Makes the background preload truly invisible: nothing you set up by hand gets overwritten, other windows stop flickering, and the viewport stays smooth while layers preload. Follow-up to the shipped View-Layer Preload.

docs.title: Context — why
docs.text: Preloading works, but it restores your view by re-running the cascade instead of putting back what was actually there. If your camera, world, or an action was set by hand, each preload tick silently overwrites it — and a layer that resolves no camera clears yours. Ticks also run during playback, renders and navigation (stutter), and scenes shown in a second window are left on whatever take was preloaded last. That's the "looks weird in the viewport" report.

docs.title: What it does (after this change)
docs.text:
• What you see before a preload run is exactly what you see during and after it — manual camera/world/action choices included.
  • Preload politely waits while you play animation, render, or navigate, and paces itself so heavy layers don't freeze the interface.
  • Scenes visible in other windows are put back exactly as they were.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text: Before touching a scene, the preloader takes a small snapshot of the real state (names only — camera, world, compositor, each object's action). After each layer builds, it writes that snapshot back verbatim, skipping values that already match, instead of re-resolving the cascade. Ticks skip while Blender is busy (playback / render job / modal navigation) and the tick interval adapts to how heavy the last layer was.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Snapshot/restore engine: capture raw state per touched scene; guarded verbatim restore each tick (replaces the re-cascade restore)
  • Tick gating: pause during animation playback, render jobs, and modal navigation; adaptive tick interval
  • Freshness + draw cost: use the rest-cache freshness check in start; compute panel row states once per redraw
  • Pacing preference (Gentle / Balanced / Fast) + idle-settle: builds fire only after the viewport has been still
  • Progress detail: total bar in the panel, now-building row, per-layer build times
  • Tests + in-app verify — confirmed working in-app 2026-07-12 (leak, lag, lock, progress all good)

docs.title: Open questions (with leans)
docs.text:
1. Drop the forced re-evaluation on restore? → Yes — guarded writes mean an unchanged layer costs nothing; when something did change, the next redraw pays the same cached ~50 ms once.
  2. Gate on modal operators too (navigation)? → Yes — it's a one-line check and directly fixes the orbit stutter.
  3. When to restore a background scene's settings? → Every tick (guarded writes make it near-free), so a second window never shows a foreign take.

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• Automated: snapshot round-trip (capture → mutate → restore = byte-identical names), gating decisions, guarded-write skip logic; existing preload suite stays green.
  • In-app: set a manual camera + world on a layer whose cascade resolves neither → run Preload All → both survive. Open a second window on another scene → run preload → that window never changes. Orbit during a run → no hitching. Undo after a run → single clean step, no addon steps.

docs.title: Dev companion
docs.text: → _dev/preload-viewport-integrity.md

docs.title: Preset Change Notifications
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: UX Safety · Size: 🟡

Presets can be edited by someone else — a shared preset on a network drive, or a project preset a teammate updated. Today you would never know. This notices when a preset has changed since you applied it, tells you who changed it, and lets you reload theirs, keep yours, or compare the two.

The existing warning system already spots when your own values drift away from a preset. This is the same idea pointed the other way: the preset drifting away from you. It applies to every storage tier, not only shared ones.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Remember what a preset looked like at the moment it was applied
  • Notice when the preset on disk no longer matches that
  • Record who changed a preset and when, and show it in the warning
  • Offer reload theirs, keep mine, or compare
  • Check at sensible moments only, never while drawing
  • Decide what name a person is identified by, and make it their choice
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Dev companion
docs.text: → _dev/preset-change-notifications.md

docs.title: Preset Reliability
docs.text:
In Progress

Underway right now.

Theme: Quality · Area: Core · Size: 🟢

Make presets hold up: a preset file that no longer matches the add-on is caught instead of loading silently, you can ask a preset which values it can't store, and the preset types that used to be quietly skipped are covered. Built and shipped in 0.7.9 — only the in-app confirmation is outstanding.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Catch outdated or incompatible preset files on load, instead of letting them apply silently
  • A "Check Coverage" report listing the values a preset can't store
  • Driven and NLA-only values show up in the Channels panel
  • Material, World and File-Output presets are no longer skipped by preset operations
  • Duplicating an action leaves no stray rest-pose slots behind
  • Project presets resolve to the same folder at startup and after a reload
  • Confirmed working in the app (changelog goes green)

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
• A preset saved by an older add-on version → loading it reports the mismatch rather than applying silently.
  • Run Check Coverage on a preset → the report lists the values that can't be stored.
  • A driven value and an NLA-only value both appear in the Channels panel.
  • A preset touching Material, World and a File Output node → all three are included, none skipped.
  • Duplicate an object with an action → no leftover rest-pose slots.
  • Set a project preset, restart / reload → it resolves to the same folder.

Theme: Tooling · Area: Core · Size: 🟡

Auto-clears stale bytecode on a version change, with a Reload Add-on button.

  • Clear pycache on a version change

docs.title: Precise Batch Render ETA
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Feature · Area: Render · Size: 🟡

Make the batch render ETA self-correcting and accurate to within a few percent — it learns from the takes that already finished instead of trusting a pre-batch guess for the whole run.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Live drift correction: adjust remaining estimates from how this batch's finished takes actually performed
  • Project the in-flight take's remaining time from its real render progress, not just elapsed time
  • Remember what settings a recorded render time was measured under, and rescale it when settings change
  • Smarter calibration probes that account for denoising and compositing cost
  • Keep a short history of recent render times per take instead of only the last one
  • Rethink prediction: calibration probes render at reduced resolution under real sampling settings, and the running render's own remaining-time estimate drives the live ETA

docs.title: Render Version Token
docs.text:
In Progress

Underway right now.

Theme: Feature · Area: Output · Size: 🔴

A render versioning system for output. Drop a version number into any output path or name as a token (compose it yourself — e.g. v[rev:03d] gives v001), bump it from a Version field in the Output area, and let it continue from the highest version already on disk. It grows from there: each view layer can carry its own version (advancing layer by layer), an in-version sub-counter for small iterations, a per-version note with history, the ability to lock a version as final, and a one-click jump to the newest version folder.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Per-scene version counter + a rev token (zero-paddable, e.g. [rev:03d]) that resolves in output paths and names
  • Version field + step up/down control in the Output area, with the live path preview reflecting it
  • Detect the highest version already on disk for the current output and continue from there — always the highest, never reusing a gap
  • Per-view-layer versioning: each view layer keeps and advances its own version (layer by layer); scope is a preference (per scene or per view layer)
  • Set the version padding once in preferences (v001 / v01 / v1) so the plain token uses it — no need to spell out the padding each time
  • Sub-versions for small iterations within a version (e.g. v002.03) via a second token
  • A short note per version, with a history dropdown of past versions
  • Lock a version as final so auto-advance / overwrite can't touch it
  • One-click "open the newest version folder" in the system file browser
  • Tidy up: move the versions you don't want into an "archive" folder kept right beside the version you're keeping
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Rest State Mode
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Feature · Area: Core · Size: 🟡

A mode you switch on from the navigation header, next to the lock. While it's on, every view layer shows the Rest State action — so you can see the baseline in place, compare it against your current work, and edit values to define the rest state, all without leaving the layer you're on. Turn it off and every layer goes straight back to its normal action.

The trick that keeps this safe: the mode never changes what's assigned in the tree. It only tells the cascade to read the Rest State action for the action tier while it's active, so there's nothing to undo or restore when you switch it off. Feasibility proven end-to-end (spike 27/27): objects find their own rest slots, uncaptured objects simply hold their pose, and toggling back restores everything exactly.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• The mode itself: a project-wide switch that makes the cascade read the Rest State action everywhere, tree untouched
  • Renders and rename/drift helpers are exempt — output always uses the real actions, and nothing nags or renames while the mode is on
  • The toggle: instant apply on click, autokey pauses while on, and it takes turns with Value Lock instead of fighting it
  • The ghost button in the navigation header, a badge in the tree, and tooltips
  • Safety net: warn if objects would share one rest slot, and the full test suite stays green
  • Confirmed working in the app (changelog goes green)

docs.title: Roadmap Single-Source & Live Wiki
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Tooling · Area: Wiki · Size: 🟡

Make one push publish the roadmap. The source stays here in doc/roadmap/ (status = folder); the public wiki board is generated in CI from this repo on every push, so it can't go stale. Match the current style on the wiki — Active/Ideas/Done tables and real phase checkboxes.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Add a **Size:** token to every per-feature file so the folders are the complete board source
  • Wiki: enable pymdownx.tasklist so - [ ] phases render as real checkboxes
  • Generator emits the three board tables (Theme · Area · Size · What · Link), not bullet lists
  • Move generation into the wiki CI build (runs against the cloned addon source); drop the manual local regenerate step
  • notify-wiki also fires on doc/roadmap/** changes so the board rebuilds on roadmap edits
  • doc/ROADMAP.md becomes a generated GitHub board (wiki pointer + aligned tables), kept fresh by a pre-commit hook
  • Update the roadmap skill, README, the auto-push rule, and memories to the one-push flow

Follow-up (infra, not addon code): renew the WIKI_REPO_DISPATCH_TOKEN PAT (returns 401, pre-existing) so addon-only pushes auto-publish instantly. Until then the daily cron and a manual wiki deploy still publish.

docs.title: Stale-Code Guard
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Tooling · Area: Core · Size: 🟢

Warns when the add-on's files on disk are newer than the code Blender is running, so test results are never trusted from a stale session again.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Record a code fingerprint (newest .py change time) when the add-on loads
  • Slow background check compares disk vs loaded; red navigation warning with "restart Blender" notice when they differ

docs.title: Still & Animation Mode
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Feature · Area: Core · Size: 🟡

A Still switch for the timeline, backed by one global rule. Turn Still on and the timeline collapses to a single frame — frame 0 only, start and end both pinned to 0 — so you can build and light a still without ever landing a keyframe on the wrong frame. Turn it off and the real frame range comes back exactly. Still can be flipped for the whole project from one master switch, or set on a single take so that take is a still while its neighbours keep animating. Everything else is Animation — the normal state, unchanged. Built as its own self-contained feature, mirroring Rest State Mode and Value Lock.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• The Still engine: a self-contained module that snapshots a scene's frame range, pins it to 0→0, and restores it exactly — with a guard so it never double-captures
  • The global master toggle first: one switch in the navigation header puts every take into Still at once (autokey stays on)
  • The per-take flag: a view layer can be set to Still on its own, applied the moment it becomes the active take, riding the same engine
  • Renders: a Still take renders its single frame 0; animation takes are untouched (batch render + background worker)
  • The UI: the Still toggle + a badge on the take row and the nav header, with tooltips
  • Confirmed working in the app (changelog goes green)

docs.title: Takes — Iterative Feedback Loop
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: Takes Tree · Size: 🔴

Give the addon's namesake its true meaning. What we now call a view layer's "Versions" become Takes — one attempt after another at the same layer, saved as you loop through feedback: build, show, get notes, save a take, work the changes in, show again — until it's approved. Step back through past takes any time, or circle an earlier one as the keeper. View layers stop being called takes in the process; they're simply layers.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Rename the deepest tier everywhere you see it: the Versions list becomes Takes (panel, tooltips, docs, wiki)
  • View layers stop being called takes: the render pie reads "ALL LAYERS" / "SELECTED LAYERS", the queue counts layers, and the wiki is reworded to match
  • The [version] token becomes [take], with the old spelling still working in existing paths and templates
  • A short feedback note on each take — what the review asked for that round
  • One-click "new take from here" to snapshot the current state and start the next iteration
  • Later: the render version number can follow the active take (render take 3 → v003 on disk)
  • Much later, after a safe transition period: [version] may become the render version number

docs.title: Finish moving UI wording into the texts folder
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Chore · Area: UI · Size: 🔴

The texts/ package was meant to hold every word the addon shows, so wording can be adjusted in one place and later handed to a translator. It was never finished: 619 strings still sit in the code — most of them operator tooltips that Blender takes from a developer docstring — and 31 constants in texts/ are dead weight that nothing reads. Nothing is broken today; the win is wording control and stable translation keys.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Migration script that moves a flagged string into the right texts module and points the code at it (dry-run first)
  • Pilot on the small shapes — 11 info-box bodies and 9 tooltip literals
  • Property descriptions (196)
  • Operator tooltips (403), one code area at a time, Preferences first
  • Clear the 31 orphan constants
  • Keep it at zero — the auditor flags any new hardcoded text on the next scan

Plan: dev/plan_text_centralization.md · dev notes: _dev/text-centralization.md

docs.title: Token Builder Popover
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Feature · Area: UI · Size: 🟡

Replace the open→find→copy→close→paste→repeat token loop with one popover that opens already filtered to the tokens valid for the field you're editing. A field at the top is seeded with the field's current value and stays manually editable; a search field filters the token list live as you type; click tokens to append; reorder or remove individual tokens with a chip row; then copy the assembled pattern or apply it back to the field.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Buffer + popover shell: builder field pinned at the top (seeded from the target field), action row (Copy · Apply · Clear), stays open across clicks
  • Live search field below the builder: type to instantly filter the matching tokens
  • Context-filtered token grid: show only tokens valid for the invoking field; click appends to the builder field
  • Token chips + reorder: parse the builder into chips; click to select, ◄ ► to move a token, ✕ to remove any token
  • Output: Copy the assembled pattern; Apply replaces the target field (seeded, so non-destructive) where it's safe to write back
  • Wire launch buttons — Smart Output (Properties Output panel), naming-template prefs, and the node-editor panel all wired; every token field carries its own seeded Build Syntax button
  • Mega sheet: factor the token grid into one context-filtered component (takes a context key, shows only valid tokens) and migrate the Naming browser onto it — one grid, each surface keeps its own shell. Unblocks Token Favorites (built once on the shared grid). Done: token_builder.categorized_tokens + TOKEN_CATEGORY_MODEL + 'NAMING' filter; popover, node-editor accordion, and Naming browser all migrated. Plus a richer hover tooltip (description + live value) and a token/value toggle by the search box so the user can see where each token pulls data from. 583 tests — confirmed working in-app
  • Chips / Field mode toggle: mutually-exclusive views (movable chips vs raw editable field); uniform 4×2 token grid; search filters at 3+ chars — confirmed working in-app
  • Upper-strip redesign: merged title+mode header; live resolved-name preview line (✓/⚠ validity); two-line resolved chips (token + what it resolves to) — confirmed working in-app
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog — logic tests pass; popover confirmed working in-app (keep-open, modes, grid, search); changelog written (Added #2)

docs.title: Token Favorites
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: UI · Size: 🟡

Let users mark their most-used tokens as favorites and flip the token sheet to a "favorites only" view, so the tokens they actually need are front and centre instead of buried in the full list. The favorites set is saved in the addon preferences so it sticks across sessions and files. There are two token sheets — Naming and Output — and favorites should work the same on both, sharing one favorites set.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Favorite toggle on each token (star/pin to add or remove), on both sheets
  • Persist one shared favorites set in the addon preferences (survives restart)
  • "Show favorites only" toggle on each token sheet
  • Sensible behaviour when favorites is empty (fall back to the full list, or a hint)
  • Make it play nicely with the live search and context filter (favorites ∩ search ∩ context)
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Find and Replace Tokens
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: Output · Size: 🟡

Rename a token and every path or template still using the old name is left behind. This finds a token everywhere it is used in the current file and swaps it for another, in one step, with a preview of exactly what will change before anything is touched.

Useful whenever you rename your own tokens, which is far more common than any of the repair cases — and it is the tool you reach for when a file needs bringing in line with a token that was renamed somewhere else.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Find every use of a token across the current file, listed by where it lives
  • Replace it with another token, or with plain text
  • Preview every change before applying, with a way to skip individual ones
  • Apply as a single undo step
  • Say plainly that only this file is covered
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Icon Sheet UX Polish
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: UX · Area: Preferences · Size: 🟢

Two quality-of-life touches for the category icon sheet in the custom-tokens manager. First, the name of the currently chosen icon gets a copy button beside it, so the icon's identifier can be grabbed with one click and pasted anywhere. Second, the sheet opens smarter: instead of always landing on the first category, it opens with the category of the already-chosen icon selected, so the current pick is immediately in view.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Copy button beside the current icon's name (one click puts the identifier on the clipboard)
  • Sheet opens on the category that contains the currently chosen icon
  • In-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Override Built-in Tokens
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: Output · Size: 🟡

Let a custom token take over a built-in token's name and redefine what it means. Name your own token camera and yours is what gets used; the built-in steps aside. Useful when the built-in reads the wrong thing for your setup, and it also means a future built-in can never silently change what your existing files produce.

Because a silenced built-in is invisible by nature, the token panel shows how many are currently overridden and which ones, so it is never a surprise.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• A custom token may reuse a built-in name instead of being rejected
  • The custom definition wins, and the built-in steps aside
  • Mark every overriding token as an override, everywhere tokens are listed
  • Hovering an override explains what it replaces and what the built-in did
  • Show how many built-ins are currently overridden, and which
  • Reword the existing clash warning from an error into an override notice
  • Make sure the addon's own internal uses don't depend on a name a user can redefine
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Dev companion
docs.text: → _dev/token-override-builtins.md

docs.title: Token Preset Ownership
docs.text:
In Progress

Underway right now.

Theme: Architecture · Area: Preferences · Size: 🟡

Custom tokens are currently stored twice — as preset files, and as a second copy inside the preferences file — and the two drift apart, which has already cost real data (category icons emptied, a token set with no preset recorded against it). Make the preset file the only home for tokens, and make the choice of preset belong to the project: which token set you are using is remembered in the blend file, not in preferences. Preferences only offers a starting choice, used by files that haven't picked one yet.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Stop writing token definitions into the preferences file
  • Remember the chosen token preset in the blend file, one choice per file
  • Use the preferences starting choice only when a file hasn't picked one
  • Load the resulting token set when a file opens
  • Keep personal choices (favourites, hidden, recent icons) in preferences where they belong
  • One-time migration so nobody loses tokens they already have
  • Token edits mark the preset as changed, saved or reverted like other presets
  • Require a preset before tokens can be created
  • "Download Token Examples" button pointing at the wiki, so no sample set ships in the addon
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Dev companion
docs.text: → _dev/token-preset-ownership.md

The dropdown for choosing a custom-token preset doesn't lay out correctly — the delete buttons and the "Save current tokens as…" entry break out into a detached second column instead of listing cleanly. Bring it in line with how every other preset picker in the addon looks and behaves: a searchable list in one tidy column, with delete handled the same way the render presets handle it.

docs.text:

  • Move delete out of the list and onto the preset row, as elsewhere
  • Add create, rename and delete buttons matching the properties preset rows

docs.title: Smart Syntax Reach & Formatting
docs.text:
In Progress

Underway right now.

Theme: Feature · Area: Output · Size: 🟡

Make Smart Syntax tokens work in more of the places where naming matters, and give the date and time tokens full custom formatting. Today tokens resolve in output paths and the naming templates; extend them so they also fill the render metadata stamp note and timeline-marker renaming. And let the date/time tokens take any format you specify, not just the fixed presets, so a stamp or filename reads exactly the date style you want.

docs.title: Token Repair Dialog
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: UX Safety · Size: 🔴

When a token used in your paths or templates no longer exists — usually after switching token presets — one dialog explains what broke and offers the ways out: leave it, strip it from the fields, or put the token back. Reached from three places: switching preset, clicking the warning on a field, and starting a batch render. Stripping a token can leave two separators stuck together, so it offers to tidy that up too, and putting several tokens back at once is quick rather than one-at-a-time.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Find which fields use a token that no longer exists, across all open scenes
  • One dialog reached from switching preset, a field warning, and starting a render
  • Leave as-is, or strip the token from the affected fields
  • Offer to close the gap when stripping leaves doubled separators
  • Put a token back: copy it from another preset, or define it fresh
  • Quick way to define several fresh tokens at once, with fast category assignment
  • Red warning on any field still holding an unknown token, reopening the dialog
  • Say plainly that only open scenes were checked
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Dev companion
docs.text: → _dev/token-repair-dialog.md

docs.title: Unresolved Token Safety
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Quality · Area: Output · Size: 🟡

If an output path uses a token that no longer exists, nothing warns you — the token text is left in the path and Blender renders into a folder literally named after it. Worse, an unresolved token is always the same text, so if it was the part that made your outputs differ, every output collapses onto one path and the render overwrites its own work. Make that impossible to miss: log it, show it, and check before a batch render starts rather than after it finishes.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Tell apart "token doesn't exist", "no scene to read from", and "reading it failed"
  • Leave the unresolved token in the user's own bracket style, so it can still be repaired
  • Log a clear warning whenever a token can't be resolved
  • Check before a batch render starts, and report which outputs would collide
  • Treat cosmetic uses (stamp note) as a warning, not a blocker
  • Tests, in-app verification, changelog

docs.title: Dev companion
docs.text: → _dev/unresolved-token-safety.md

docs.title: Value Lock
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Feature · Area: Core · Size: 🔴

Cinema 4D-style lock to prevent accidental edits. Blender can't reject an edit, so instead a locked value snaps straight back to the value it had when you locked it. Works on any view layer, with any action assigned — or with no action at all — and only ever acts while the lock is switched on.

The lock protects the scene's UNKEYED values on your selection: any static value you nudge snaps back to what it was when you locked — guarding against accidental global changes. Keyframed (animated) values stay fully unlocked; deliberately keying a value takes it out of the lock (autokey is paused while locked). Take switching keeps working while locked. (C4D-style: it guards the static setup, never the animation.)

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• One lock button in the navigation header, between Autokey and Timeline Sync
  • Locking captures the whole scene — everything that can hold a static value (objects, bones, modifiers, data, shape keys, materials, world)
  • Only your selected objects are enforced, so take switching and the cascade are never fought
  • Snap unkeyed values back when the drag is released; keyframed values stay fully unlocked
  • Make it work on any VL regardless of action type, and with no action assigned
  • Fold the snap-back into one undo step; unlocking releases everything
  • Pause autokey while locked (button dims) — it would otherwise slip every nudge past the lock; deliberate manual keying still opts a channel out

docs.title: Variant Switch — Per-Surface Material Channels
docs.text:
Planned

On the roadmap, not started yet.

Theme: Feature · Area: Variants · Size: 🔴

Let one material pool cover several surfaces at once, so an object whose slots use different materials from the same pool switches each surface independently instead of flattening them all to a single material.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Split a pool's materials into channels, one per surface
  • Keep a material to a single channel (no adding it to two, or twice to one)
  • Give each variant its own pick per channel
  • On switch, move each slot to its own channel's material — never collapse distinct surfaces into one
  • Carry existing single-pool setups across unchanged (one default channel behaves exactly like today)
  • Show the channels in the Variants tree
  • Never silently apply the wrong material when a variant's picks are out of sync

docs.title: Variant Tokens for Smart Output
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Feature · Area: Render · Size: 🟢

Variant name, tag, and index tokens for Smart Output file paths.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• variant / variant_tag / variant_index tokens
  • Resolve the active cascade variant into the path

docs.title: Blender Version Guard
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Quality · Area: Core · Size: 🟡

Warns in the side panel when Blender is older than the supported version.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Detect an unsupported version on startup
  • Warning panel with the version info
  • N-panel header alert icon

docs.title: View-Layer Preload
docs.text:
Shipped

Done and in the add-on.

Theme: Performance · Area: Core · Size: 🟡

Pre-builds the view layers of every scene so the first switch to each take is instant instead of lagging. Choose Automatic, Manual, or Off.

docs.title: Phases
docs.text:
• Add a "View-Layer Preload" mode setting — Automatic / Manual / Off — in Automations preferences
  • Build the preload engine: prepare each view layer in the background without disturbing the one you're on (no flicker)
  • Preload every scene's view layers (not just the active scene)
  • Preload the full take (cascade), so the first visit is instant, not just the second
  • Automatic mode: preload shortly after a file opens
  • Off mode: no automatic preload and no badge
  • Navigation panel: red badge → panel with progress bar, ETA, per-layer state (Active/Ready/Queued/Cold) + Preload/Cancel
  • Nested collapsible subpanels (scene-group / scene / vl-group) with a Preload button at every level
  • In-app check: first switch to a preloaded take is instant, no flicker, undo still clean (unit tests done)

docs.title: Workflows
docs.text:
Step-by-step tutorials for real production scenarios.

  • Animation Variants

    Set up different animation takes per View Layer — turntables, tilts, explosions — each with its own camera and output.

    Animation Variants

  • Material Variants

    Use Variant Switch for product colour, finish, and material options — each Part draws from its own material pool, with an optional preset per entry.

    Material Variants

  • Batch Rendering

    Render every variant and angle in one go — foreground or background, with Smart Output naming and the cascade applied per View Layer.

    Batch Rendering

docs.title: Workflow: Animation Variants
docs.text: This workflow shows how to set up different animation takes per View Layer — for example, a product spinning, tilting, and exploding, each on its own View Layer with independent cameras.

docs.title: 1. Create View Layers
docs.text:
Create one View Layer per animation state:

  1. Open the Takes Tree.
  2. Click + → Add View Layer for each animation.
  3. Name them descriptively: "Spin", "Tilt", "Explode".

docs.title: 2. Group Them
docs.text:
1. Select the first View Layer.
  2. Press Ctrl+G to create a group called "Animations".
  3. Move the other View Layers into this group.

docs.title: 3. Assign Cameras
docs.text:
Each animation likely needs its own camera angle:

  1. Click the camera icon on each View Layer row.
  2. Assign the appropriate camera.

docs.title: 4. Animate Per View Layer
docs.text:
1. Switch to "Spin" View Layer by clicking it in the tree.
  2. The cascade automatically assigns a dedicated action for this View Layer.
  3. Animate your objects. Keyframes are stored in the View Layer-specific action.
  4. Switch to "Tilt" — the objects snap back to their Rest State.
  5. Create a different animation on this View Layer.

Rest State

The Rest State system ensures objects return to their default pose when switching away from an animated View Layer. This happens automatically.

docs.title: 5. Render All Animations
docs.text:
1. Enable multi-select in the tree sidebar — the Multi-Select toggle in the tree's statistics row, which adds a checkbox to every row.
  2. Select all animation View Layers.
  3. Click the Render button.
  4. Smart Output names each output file using the View Layer name automatically.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Each View Layer has:

  • Its own camera angle
  • Its own animation action
  • Its own output file name
  • All managed from one unified tree

docs.title: Workflow: Batch Rendering
docs.text: This workflow covers setting up and running a full batch render across multiple View Layers with cascade overrides and Smart Output.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Scene with multiple View Layers configured
  • Cascade overrides assigned (cameras, worlds, presets)
  • Smart Output patterns defined

docs.title: 1. Define Output Pattern
docs.text:
1. Go to Properties > Output.
  2. Enable Smart Output.

  3. Set the Directory:

    //renders/[scene]/[view_layer]/

  4. Set the File Name:

    [view_layer]_[camera]_####.[file_format]

docs.title: 2. Assign Render Presets
docs.text:
For consistent quality across all View Layers:

  1. Configure your render settings (engine, samples, resolution).
  2. Save as a render preset via the cascade popover.
  3. Assign it at the Global level for all View Layers, or per View Layer Group for different quality tiers.

docs.title: Foreground Mode
docs.text:
1. Open the Takes Tree sidebar.
  2. Click the render button to open the render menu, make sure Render Mode is set to Foreground, then pick a scope row (e.g. Selected Takes → This Scene).
  3. The queue processes each View Layer in tree order:
    • Switches scene/View Layer context
    • Applies cascade overrides (camera, world, action, variants)
    • Applies render preset
    • Renders and saves to the Smart Output path
  4. Progress shows per-View Layer in the render queue list.

docs.title: Background Mode
docs.text:
1. Open the same render menu, switch Render Mode to Background, then pick a scope row.
  2. Blender stays interactive while renders run in background processes.
  3. Tree view thumbnails update as each View Layer completes.

docs.title: Monitoring Progress
docs.text:
The render queue sidebar shows:

  • Per-View Layer progress bar
  • Status indicator (Pending, Rendering, Saving, Done, Failed, Cancelled)
  • Failed items show a tooltip with the error reason

The queue's time estimates self-correct while a batch runs — once the first take finishes, the remaining time reflects how this run is actually performing — and Calibrate Render Times learns per-take render times ahead of a run.

docs.title: Output Structure
docs.text:
With the patterns above, your output folder looks like:

renders/
├── Kitchen/
│   ├── Front_3-4/
│   │   ├── Front_3-4_CamHero_0001.png
│   │   └── Front_3-4_CamHero_0250.png
│   └── Top_Down/
│       ├── Top_Down_CamTop_0001.png
│       └── Top_Down_CamTop_0250.png
└── Bathroom/
    └── ...

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
Completion Sound

Set a notification sound in Preferences > Workflow > Render to be alerted when background renders finish. Comes with bundled sounds, or point the Sound Folder at your own .wav files.

Preview Before Render

Use Alt-click on the render toggle at the far right of a View Layer's row in the Takes Tree (the camera icon that enables or disables the layer for rendering) to preview its last rendered output without re-rendering.

Save First

Background renders require the .blend file to be saved. Foreground renders work from the current memory state.

docs.title: Workflow: Material Variants
docs.text: This workflow shows how to set up product color/finish variants using the Variant Switch system.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text: You have a product (e.g., a water bottle) that needs to be rendered in three finishes: Matte Black, Brushed Aluminum, and Rose Gold.

docs.title: 1. Create the Product
docs.text:
1. Open the Variant Switch panel.
  2. Click + to create a new Product named "Bottle".
  3. A default Part ("base") is created automatically.

docs.title: 2. Define Parts
docs.text:
Split the product into components:

  1. Add a Part called "Body" → assign the body collection.
  2. Add a Part called "Cap" → assign the cap collection.
  3. Add a Part called "Label" → assign the label collection.

docs.title: 3. Add Materials to Pools
docs.text:
For each Part, populate the material pool:

  1. Expand "Body" to see its pool.
  2. Assign Mat_Matte_Black to slot 1.
  3. Assign Mat_Aluminum to slot 2 (auto-creates).
  4. Assign Mat_Rose_Gold to slot 3 (auto-creates).
  5. Repeat for Cap and Label with their respective materials.

docs.title: 4. Create States
docs.text:
Add a State for each variant:

  1. Add State "Matte Black" → set pool index 1 for all parts.
  2. Add State "Aluminum" → set pool index 2 for all parts.
  3. Add State "Rose Gold" → set pool index 3 for all parts.

docs.title: Previewing
docs.text: Click the diamond icon on any State to instantly see that variant in the viewport.

docs.title: Option A: Manual
docs.text:
1. Activate each State.
  2. Render with F12.

docs.title: Option B: Cascade Integration
docs.text:
1. Create a View Layer per variant.
  2. In each View Layer's cascade, assign the matching Variant Switch state — click the variant cascade icon on the tree row (the same UV-sync icon as the Globals Variants mode) to open its popover and pick the state.
  3. Batch Render all View Layers in one go.

docs.title: Option C: Combined with Camera Angles
docs.text:
Create a View Layer for each combination (variant × angle):

View Layer Variant Camera Black_Front Matte Black Cam_Front Black_Side Matte Black Cam_Side Alu_Front Aluminum Cam_Front Alu_Side Aluminum Cam_Side

Batch Render handles all combinations with Smart Output tokens:

[variant]_[camera]_####.[file_format]
